# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Breakdown of the Disruption Mechanism

The disruption of a microbial cell in a bead mill is the **superposition of four physical mechanisms** occurring in parallel:

| # | Mechanism | Physical Origin | Dominant Scale |
|---|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Bead–cell collision** | Direct impact when a bead strikes a cell | Micro-scale |
| 2 | **Bead–bead collision (cavitation + shear)** | Impact between adjacent beads squeezes fluid at very high shear rate | Sub-micron |
| 3 | **Hydrodynamic shear** | Velocity gradient in the rotating liquid between beads | Micro-scale |
| 4 | **Compression / friction between beads** | Cell caught between two beads is mechanically crushed | Micro-scale |

> [!NOTE]
> For **yeast** (large, tough cell wall, 5–10 µm), mechanism **(1)** dominates. For **bacteria** (smaller, 0.5–2 µm), mechanism **(3)** — hydrodynamic shear — is the most efficient.

### 2.1.1 First-Order Disruption Kinetics (the Core Model)

The breakage of cells in a bead mill follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the *unbroken* cell concentration, mathematically analogous to an irreversible chemical reaction:

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = k \left( R_m - R \right)$$

where:

- $R$ = mass of intracellular protein released per unit volume at time $t$ *(g/L)*,
- $R_m$ = maximum releasable protein *(g/L)* — i.e., total protein in the cell at infinite disruption time,
- $k$ = first-order disruption rate constant *(min⁻¹ or s⁻¹)*,
- $t$ = residence time in the mill.

Integrating with the initial condition $R(0)=0$:

$$R(t) = R_m \left( 1 - e^{-k t} \right)$$

Rearranged for the **fractional disruption** $X = R / R_m$:

$$X = 1 - e^{-k t}$$

This is the **canonical kinetic expression** you must memorise for any KTU derivation question.

### 2.1.2 Functional Dependence of k on Operating Variables

Empirically (and supported by dimensional analysis), $k$ follows a power-law form:

$$k = K \cdot N^{a} \cdot d_b^{\,b} \cdot \phi^{c} \cdot C_{cell}^{\,d}$$

where $N$ = agitator speed, $d_b$ = bead diameter, $\phi$ = bead loading, $C_{cell}$ = cell concentration, and $a, b, c, d$ are **positive empirical exponents** (typically $a \approx 1$–$2$, $b \approx -1$ to $+1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why small beads are not always better:** Reducing $d_b$ increases the *number* of beads per unit volume (good for collision frequency), but it also reduces the *kinetic energy per bead* (because $E_{kin} \propto d_b^{3}$). The optimum bead diameter is therefore an **engineering trade-off** — usually **0.4 – 0.7 mm** for yeast, **0.1 – 0.3 mm** for bacteria.

## 2.2 Design Considerations

### 2.2.1 Geometric Design
- **Chamber geometry:** cylindrical, jacketed, aspect ratio 2:1 to 4:1.
- **Agitator types:**
  - **Disk (turbo) agitator** — best for low-viscosity yeast/bacterial suspensions.
  - **Pin agitator** — high turbulence, used for viscous mycelial cultures.
  - **Annular (conical) gap mill** — very high shear, used for microalgae and small bacteria.
- **Rotor–stator gap:** typically **1 – 5 mm**; tighter gaps give higher local shear.

### 2.2.2 Bead Selection
- **Glass beads** — cheap, chemically inert, low density; suitable for lab scale.
- **Zirconium silicate (ZrSiO₄)** — high density, low wear, industry standard.
- **Steel shot** — used for pigments and very tough cells, but contaminates with metal ions.

### 2.2.3 Thermal & Sanitary Design
- **Double-wall jacket** with chilled water/glycol (5–10 °C inlet) for heat removal.
- **316L stainless steel** contact surfaces, electro-polished to Ra ≤ 0.4 µm.
- **Sterile air overlay / N₂ blanket** to prevent oxidative damage to product.

### 2.2.4 Operating Mode
- **Batch mode:** cells + beads recirculated in a stirred tank until target release achieved.
- **Single-pass continuous mode:** feed pumped once through the mill at residence time $\tau = V_{liq}/Q$.
- **Recirculation (multi-pass) mode:** most common at production scale; enables fine control of fractional release by varying the number of passes $n_p$.

## 2.3 Mixing Characteristics — The Dimensionless Framework

The mixing behaviour in a bead mill is characterised by classical impeller power-draw correlations adapted to dense suspensions.

### 2.3.1 Power Number
$$N_p \;=\; \frac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}}$$

where $P$ = power drawn by agitator, $\rho$ = suspension density, $D$ = agitator diameter.

In the **turbulent regime** ($Re > 10^4$), $N_p$ is approximately constant (the "Newtonian plateau"):

$$N_p^{\,\text{turb}} \;\approx\; 5\text{–}8 \quad \text{(disk agitator in dense bead suspension)}$$

### 2.3.2 Reynolds Number
$$Re \;=\; \frac{\rho \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu}$$

For bead mill suspensions, $Re$ is typically **> 10⁴** (fully turbulent).

### 2.3.3 Froude Number (gravity effects)
$$Fr \;=\; \frac{N^{2} \, D}{g}$$

At high $\phi$ and high $N$, $Fr$ may need to be incorporated to correct for bead sedimentation/coronation effects.

### 2.3.4 Impeller Tip Speed
$$v_{tip} \;=\; \pi \, N \, D$$

A practical rule of thumb is $v_{tip} = 5\text{–}20$ m/s for yeast disruption.

### 2.3.5 Péclet Number (axial mixing in continuous mode)
$$Pe \;=\; \frac{u \, L}{D_{ax}}$$

For high bead loading $\phi > 0.7$, the suspension approaches **plug-flow** ($Pe \to \infty$), which is ideal for first-order disruption kinetics.

### 2.3.6 Specific Energy Input (energy per kg of suspension)
$$\bar{E} \;=\; \frac{P \cdot t}{m_{susp}} \;=\; \frac{P}{Q \cdot \rho}$$

This is the **key scale-up criterion** — equal $\bar{E}$ gives equal fractional disruption regardless of mill size.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarises **every equation** you are likely to need in a KTU 2024 Module 1 examination on this topic. Master these and you will solve 95 % of the numerical parts.

| # | Equation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|----------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $R(t) = R_m \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | $R$, $R_m$ in g/L; $t$ in s or min; $k$ in s⁻¹ or min⁻¹ | Disruption kinetics |
| 2 | $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right] = k t$ | — | Linearised disruption plot |
| 3 | $k = K N^{a} d_b^{b} \phi^{c}$ | $N$ = rpm; $d_b$ = mm; $\phi$ = — | Effect of operating variables |
| 4 | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}}$ | $P$ in W; $\rho$ in kg/m³; $N$ in s⁻¹; $D$ in m | Power consumption |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ in Pa·s | Flow regime |
| 6 | $Fr = \dfrac{N^{2} D}{g}$ | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Gravity correction |
| 7 | $v_{tip} = \pi N D$ | in m/s | Agitator speed check |
| 8 | $Pe = \dfrac{u L}{D_{ax}}$ | $u$ in m/s; $L$, $D_{ax}$ in m²/s | Axial mixing |
| 9 | $\bar{E} = \dfrac{P}{Q \rho}$ | J/kg | Scale-up criterion |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{Q}$ | s or min | Residence time (continuous) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit trap:** When computing $N_p$, the **rotational speed $N$ must be in rad/s (or rev/s)**, *not* in rpm. Convert using $N_{\text{rad/s}} = 2\pi \, N_{\text{rpm}}/60$. Mixing this up is the most common mistake in KTU numericals.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Disruption Model

### Step 1 — Statement of the Rate Hypothesis

We postulate that the **rate at which intact cells are disrupted** is proportional to the **concentration of still-intact cells**. If $C(t)$ is the concentration of unbroken cells (g dry cell weight per L) and $C_0$ is the initial concentration, then:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} \;=\; -k \, C(t)$$

### Step 2 — Separation of Variables and Integration

Rearranging and integrating from $t=0$ (where $C = C_0$) to an arbitrary time $t$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\int_{0}^{t} k \, dt$$

$$\ln C(t) - \ln C_0 \;=\; -k t$$

$$C(t) \;=\; C_0 \, e^{-k t}$$

### Step 3 — Convert from "Cells Broken" to "Protein Released"

The intracellular product released $R(t)$ is directly proportional to the number of cells broken, i.e.:

$$R(t) \;=\; R_m \left[ 1 - \frac{C(t)}{C_0} \right]$$

Substituting the exponential for $C(t)$:

$$R(t) \;=\; R_m \left[ 1 - e^{-k t} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Linearised Form (useful for experimental plotting)

Rearranging the equation from Step 3 to isolate $k$:

$$e^{-k t} \;=\; 1 - \frac{R(t)}{R_m}$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-k t \;=\; \ln\!\left[ 1 - \frac{R(t)}{R_m} \right]$$

Or, equivalently, in the form most often used in KTU papers:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left[ \frac{R_m}{R_m - R(t)} \right] \;=\; k \, t\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A plot of $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right]$ versus $t$ must be a **straight line through the origin** with slope $k$. Any systematic curvature indicates either thermal denaturation of the product (at long $t$) or insufficient bead loading (at short $t$).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power-Number Correlation

The mechanical power delivered by an impeller in a Newtonian fluid is the product of the **torque $T$** on the agitator shaft and the **angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N$**:

$$P \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; 2\pi N \, T$$

In a fully baffled turbulent tank, dimensional analysis (using $\rho$, $N$, $D$, $\mu$, $g$ as the governing parameters) yields the Buckingham-$\Pi$ groups:

$$\Pi_1 \;=\; \frac{P}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}} \;\equiv\; N_p \quad\text{(Power number)}$$

$$\Pi_2 \;=\; \frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu} \;\equiv\; Re \quad\text{(Reynolds number)}$$

$$\Pi_3 \;=\; \frac{N^{2} D}{g} \;\equiv\; Fr \quad\texttext{(Froude number)}$$

Therefore the functional relationship is:

$$N_p \;=\; f(Re, Fr)$$

For **un-gassed, baffled systems with $Re > 10^{4}$**, surface gravity effects are negligible and:

$$N_p \;\longrightarrow\; \text{constant}$$

For a Rushton-type disk turbine in water this constant is **≈ 5.5**; for a 6-blade disk in a dense bead suspension it rises to **≈ 7–8** because the beads impose additional momentum transfer.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU-Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:**
> A continuous bead mill operating at steady state processes **200 L/h** of a *S. cerevisiae* suspension (dry cell weight = **40 g/L**, intracellular total soluble protein = **45 % w/w of DCW**). The mill chamber holds **3.0 L** of liquid volume. The agitator (disk, $D = 0.08$ m) runs at **3 000 rpm** in a fully turbulent regime ($N_p = 7.2$). Suspension density $\rho = 1{,}020$ kg/m³. Measured outlet protein concentration $R_{out} = 14.8$ g/L.
>
> **(i)** Calculate the residence time $\tau$.
> **(ii)** Calculate the power input $P$ and the specific energy $\bar{E}$.
> **(iii)** Calculate the maximum releasable protein $R_m$ and the disruption rate constant $k$.
> **(iv)** If the speed is raised to **3 600 rpm** and $k$ scales as $k \propto N^{1.4}$, predict the new outlet protein concentration $R_{out}'$.

### Solution — Step (i): Residence Time

$$\tau \;=\; \frac{V_{liq}}{Q} \;=\; \frac{3.0 \text{ L}}{200 \text{ L/h}} \;=\; 0.015 \text{ h} \;=\; 54 \text{ s}$$

### Solution — Step (ii): Power and Specific Energy

Convert rotational speed to s⁻¹:

$$N \;=\; \frac{3000}{60} \;=\; 50 \text{ rev/s}$$

Power input:

$$P \;=\; N_p \, \rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}$$

$$P \;=\; 7.2 \times 1020 \times (50)^{3} \times (0.08)^{5}$$

$$(50)^{3} \;=\; 125{,}000$$

$$(0.08)^{5} \;=\; 3.28 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P \;=\; 7.2 \times 1020 \times 125{,}000 \times 3.28 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P \;\approx\; 7.2 \times 1020 \times 0.410 \;=\; 3010 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 3.0 \text{ kW}$$

Specific energy per kg of suspension:

$$\bar{E} \;=\; \frac{P}{Q \cdot \rho} \;=\; \frac{3010 \text{ W}}{200/3600 \text{ kg/s} \cdot 1020 \text{ kg/m}^{3}} \;\approx\; 53.1 \text{ J/kg}$$

### Solution — Step (iii): R_m and k

Maximum releasable protein = 45 % of cell concentration:

$$R_m \;=\; 0.45 \times 40 \text{ g/L} \;=\; 18.0 \text{ g/L}$$

From the kinetic model at outlet:

$$R_{out} \;=\; R_m \left(1 - e^{-k \tau}\right)$$

$$14.8 \;=\; 18.0 \left(1 - e^{-54 k}\right)$$

$$e^{-54 k} \;=\; 1 - \frac{14.8}{18.0} \;=\; 1 - 0.8222 \;=\; 0.1778$$

$$-54 k \;=\; \ln(0.1778) \;=\; -1.727$$

$$k \;=\; \frac{1.727}{54} \;=\; 3.20 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1} \;=\; 1.92 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

### Solution — Step (iv): New Outlet Protein at 3 600 rpm

Speed ratio:

$$\frac{N'}{N} \;=\; \frac{3600}{3000} \;=\; 1.20$$

New disruption constant:

$$k' \;=\; k \left(\frac{N'}{N}\right)^{1.4} \;=\; 3.20 \times 10^{-2} \times (1.20)^{1.4}$$

$$(1.20)^{1.4} \;\approx\; 1.252$$

$$k' \;\approx\; 4.01 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

New outlet protein:

$$R_{out}' \;=\; 18.0 \left(1 - e^{-4.01 \times 10^{-2} \times 54}\right)$$

$$R_{out}' \;=\; 18.0 \left(1 - e^{-2.164}\right) \;=\; 18.0 \left(1 - 0.1148\right)$$

$$R_{out}' \;\approx\; 15.93 \text{ g/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 20 % increase in agitator speed raises protein release by **≈ 7.6 %**. This sub-linear response is precisely why bead mills are operated in *recirculation mode* at production scale — it is more energy-efficient to add residence time than to over-spin.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
> *Define bead mill disruption. List the four physical mechanisms responsible for cell rupture inside a bead mill chamber.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Bead mill disruption** is a high-energy mechanical cell disruption technique in which a dense suspension of biological cells is agitated with small grinding beads (0.1–1.5 mm diameter) inside a water-cooled chamber, rupturing the cell envelope to release intracellular products.
The four mechanisms are:
1. **Bead–cell collision** — direct kinetic impact.
2. **Bead–bead collision** — squeezing and cavitation at the contact point.
3. **Hydrodynamic shear** — velocity gradients in the fluid surrounding beads.
4. **Compression / friction between beads** — mechanical crushing of trapped cells.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four mechanisms — ½ mark each.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
> *State the first-order disruption kinetics equation for a bead mill and explain the significance of the rate constant $k$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The disruption kinetics in a bead mill follows the **first-order model**:

$$R(t) \;=\; R_m \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$$

or equivalently in linearised form:

$$\ln\!\left[ \frac{R_m}{R_m - R(t)} \right] \;=\; k \, t$$

where $R$ = protein released, $R_m$ = maximum releasable protein, $t$ = residence time, and $k$ = first-order disruption rate constant (s⁻¹ or min⁻¹).
**Significance of $k$:** It is the **engineering performance metric** of the mill — it encapsulates the combined effect of agitator speed, bead size, bead loading and cell concentration. Larger $k$ → faster disruption → smaller required residence time → higher throughput.

*(Allocation: 1 mark each for equation, definition of variables, and significance of $k$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the working of a continuous bead mill. Discuss the role of (i) bead size, (ii) bead material, and (iii) agitator speed on the disruption efficiency.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the first-order disruption kinetic model for a bead mill from the underlying rate hypothesis. Show how the disruption rate constant $k$ scales with agitator speed $N$ and bead diameter $d_b$, and discuss the engineering trade-off between bead size and specific energy input.

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

1. **Schematic (2 marks):** Draw a labelled diagram showing feed inlet, jacketed chamber, rotating disk agitator, bead bed (80 % packing), outlet screen (separating beads), and cooling water inlet/outlet. *Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1.*

2. **Working principle (1 mark):** The cell suspension is pumped through the annular chamber packed with beads; the agitator fluidises the bed, generating high local shear and impacts that rupture cells. The outlet screen retains beads while the disrupted slurry exits.

3. **Role of bead size (1.5 marks):** Small beads (≤ 0.2 mm) provide **high collision frequency** but **low kinetic energy per impact** ($E_{kin} \propto d_b^{3}$). Large beads (≥ 0.8 mm) give higher per-impact energy but lower frequency. The **optimum is typically 0.4 – 0.7 mm** for yeast.

4. **Role of bead material (1 mark):** Higher-density beads (zirconium silicate, $\rho = 3.8$ g/cm³) deliver higher impact energy at the same $N$ than glass ($\rho = 2.5$ g/cm³), but cause more wear on the chamber and are costlier. Material must be chemically inert to avoid contamination.

5. **Role of agitator speed (1.5 marks):** Higher $N$ increases both the collision frequency and the agitator tip speed, but only as $k \propto N^{a}$ with $a \approx 1$–$2$ (sub-linear). Excessive speed causes **bead centrifugation** (loss of bed fluidisation), heat generation, and product denaturation.

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

1. **Rate hypothesis (1 mark):** $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C(t)$, where $C$ = intact cell concentration.

2. **Integration (2 marks):** $\displaystyle C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$, hence $R(t) = R_m (1 - e^{-kt})$.

3. **Linearised form (1 mark):** $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right] = k t$.

4. **Functional dependence of $k$ (2 marks):** Empirical correlation $k = K N^{a} d_b^{b}$. Typical exponents: $a \approx 1.4$, $b \approx -0.5$ for yeast. Thus $k \propto N^{1.4} d_b^{-0.5}$.

5. **Engineering trade-off (1 mark):** Specific energy input $\bar{E} \propto N^{3}$. Reducing $d_b$ lowers $k$ faster than it lowers $E$, so a *minimum* bead diameter exists below which disruption becomes energetically unfavourable.

---

### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the design considerations for an industrial-scale bead mill used in bioprocessing. Compare disk, pin and annular-gap agitator designs with respect to (i) mixing intensity, (ii) heat removal, and (iii) suitability for different cell types.

**(b) [7 marks]** A continuous bead mill processes a yeast suspension at the following conditions: agitator diameter $D = 0.10$ m, speed $N = 50$ rev/s, power number $N_p = 7.5$, suspension density $\rho = 1{,}050$ kg/m³, residence time $\tau = 60$ s. The feed cell concentration is $40$ g DCW/L with intracellular protein = $45$ % w/w. The outlet soluble protein concentration is measured as $14.5$ g/L.
*Calculate:* (i) power input, (ii) maximum releasable protein $R_m$, (iii) disruption rate constant $k$, (iv) specific energy input $\bar{E}$, and (v) the agitator speed $N'$ required to achieve $95$ % disruption in the same residence time.

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

| Agitator Type | Mixing Intensity | Heat Removal | Best Suited For |
|---------------|------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Disk (Rushton-type)** | Moderate, bulk turbulence | Good (large surface area) | Bacteria, yeast, low-viscosity broths |
| **Pin** | High local turbulence near pins | Moderate (pins disturb boundary layer) | Filamentous fungi, mycelial cultures |
| **Annular / Conical gap** | Very high shear in the narrow gap | Limited (small wetted area) | Microalgae, very small bacteria |

Design considerations (additional 3.5 marks to make 7):
- **Chamber aspect ratio** 2:1 to 4:1 to ensure bead retention.
- **Jacketed cooling** to maintain $T < 30$ °C.
- **Sterile air/N₂ overlay** to prevent oxidative damage.
- **316L SS, Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** for hygienic operation.
- **Rotor–stator gap** 1 – 5 mm; smaller gap → higher shear → more disruption but more heat.
- **CIP/SIP compatibility** for batch-to-batch sterility.

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**(i) Power input**

$$P = N_p \rho N^{3} D^{5} = 7.5 \times 1050 \times (50)^{3} \times (0.10)^{5}$$

$$= 7.5 \times 1050 \times 125{,}000 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P = 7.5 \times 1050 \times 1.25 = 9843.75 \text{ W} \approx 9.84 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Maximum releasable protein**

$$R_m = 0.45 \times 40 = 18.0 \text{ g/L}$$

**(iii) Disruption rate constant $k$**

$$14.5 = 18.0 \left(1 - e^{-60 k}\right) \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-60k} = 1 - 14.5/18.0 = 0.1944$$

$$-60 k = \ln(0.1944) = -1.638 \;\Rightarrow\; k = 2.73 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(iv) Specific energy input**

$$\bar{E} = \frac{P}{Q \rho} = P \cdot \frac{\tau}{V_{liq} \rho}$$

If we assume $V_{liq} = Q \tau$, then $\bar{E} = \dfrac{P \tau}{V_{liq} \rho} = \dfrac{P}{Q \rho}$. Using $\bar{E} = P / (\rho \cdot V_{liq}/\tau) = (P\tau)/(V_{liq}\rho)$, and taking $V_{liq} = 5$ L (typical mill volume):

$$\bar{E} = \frac{9843.75 \times 60}{5 \times 1050} = \frac{590625}{5250} \approx 112.5 \text{ J/kg}$$

**(v) Speed for 95 % disruption**

For 95 % disruption: $R = 0.95 R_m = 17.1$ g/L.

$$1 - e^{-k' \tau} = 0.95 \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-k' \tau} = 0.05$$

$$k' \tau = -\ln(0.05) = 2.996 \;\Rightarrow\; k' = 2.996/60 = 4.99 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Using $k \propto N^{1.4}$:

$$\frac{k'}{k} = \left(\frac{N'}{N}\right)^{1.4} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{4.99}{2.73} = \left(\frac{N'}{50}\right)^{1.4}$$

$$1.828 = \left(\frac{N'}{50}\right)^{1.4}$$

$$\frac{N'}{50} = 1.828^{1/1.4} = 1.828^{0.714} \approx 1.534$$

$$N' \approx 76.7 \text{ rev/s} \;=\; 4600 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** of $N$ from rpm to rev/s when computing $P$ — this alone costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error** in the kinetic integration — writing $R(t) = R_m (1 + e^{-kt})$ instead of $(1 - e^{-kt})$ yields physically meaningless results. Check at $t \to \infty$: $R \to R_m$.
> 3. **Mixing $R$ with $X$** — students often compute fractional release $X$ and then forget to multiply back by $R_m$ to get protein concentration. Always end your answer in **g/L**, not dimensionless.
> 4. **Neglecting the linearised form** of the kinetics — KTU examiners frequently give full credit only when the linearised equation is derived, not just the exponential form.
> 5. **Ignoring bead sedimentation** at very low $N$ — if $Fr < 0.1$, the power correlation breaks down. Mention this in descriptive answers for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 1 viva / written exams:**

- ☐ Bead mill disruption is a **mechanical, high-energy** method using a **dense bead bed (60–90 % packing)**.
- ☐ Four disruption mechanisms: **bead–cell collision, bead–bead collision, hydrodynamic shear, compression**.
- ☐ Kinetic model is **first-order**: $R(t) = R_m(1 - e^{-kt})$; linearised: $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{R_m}{R_m-R}\right] = kt$.
- ☐ The disruption constant $k$ scales as $k \propto N^{a} d_b^{b} \phi^{c}$ — **empirical exponents are positive for $N$ and $\phi$**, can be negative for $d_b$ at large sizes.
- ☐ Power draw: $P = N_p \rho N^{3} D^{5}$. In turbulent regime, $N_p \approx 5.5$ (Rushton) to $8$ (dense bead bed).
- ☐ Impeller tip speed $v_{tip} = \pi N D$ is a quick rule-of-thumb check (5–20 m/s for yeast).
- ☐ **Scale-up criterion = constant specific energy input $\bar{E}$**, not constant $N$ or constant $P/V$.
- ☐ **Continuous mode** residence time $\tau = V_{liq}/Q$; multi-pass recirculation gives finer control.
- ☐ **Heat removal is critical** — keep broth $T < 30$ °C via jacketed cooling to protect labile products.
- ☐ **Bead choice trade-off:** density vs wear vs cost. Optimum for yeast ≈ **0.5 mm zirconium silicate at 80 % loading**.
- ☐ Bead mills are **preferred over homogenisers for yeast and fungi** because they handle high cell densities (up to 250 g DCW/L) without plugging.
- ☐ Always check units: $N$ in rev/s for $N_p$; $k$ in s⁻¹ for $\tau$ in s.
- ☐ Mention **Damköhler/Péclet number considerations** in continuous mode for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enzymatic Cell Lysis and Product Release-Basic Theory and Models, Methods and Design, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT865 - ADVANCED BIO SEPARATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Enzymatic Cell Lysis and Product Release-Basic Theory and Models, Methods and Design, Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Enzymatic Cell Lysis** is a selective, biocatalytic *primary recovery* operation in downstream bioprocessing wherein hydrolytic enzymes (e.g., lysozyme, proteinase K, zymolyase, glucanase, mannanase) cleave specific covalent bonds of the microbial cell envelope — peptidoglycan, β-glucan, mannoprotein, or lipoprotein layers — to release intracellular product (proteins, plasmid DNA, organelles, metabolites) into the surrounding aqueous phase while preserving the structural integrity and biological activity of the target molecule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Enzymatic lysis is classified as a **non-mechanical, selective disruption method** belonging to the *cell disruption* unit operation that precedes *solid–liquid separation*, *clarification*, and *chromatographic purification* in the canonical downstream sequence.

**Product Release (in lysis context)** refers to the mass-transport-limited egress of the soluble intracellular species (proteins, nucleic acids, low-molecular-weight metabolites) across the permeabilized envelope into the bulk extraction buffer, governed by Fickian diffusion, convection, and the time-dependent fractional disruption function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Vault" Analogy

Imagine a **bank vault** (microbial cell) with **three concentric doors**:

1. **Outer brass door** = Cell wall / capsule (rigid, protective)
2. **Middle phosphor-bronze door** = Plasma membrane (semi-fluid lipid bilayer)
3. **Inner steel door** = Cytoplasmic contents (proteins, DNA, metabolites)

A *mechanical lysis* technique (bead milling, French press, sonication) is like **detonating the entire vault** — fast but destructive; valuables may be shredded. An *enzymatic lysis*, by contrast, is like sending a **master locksmith (lysozyme)** with a single, perfectly shaped key that only fits the outer brass door's lock — the worker rotates the key, the bolt retracts, the outer door opens, and the *treasure inside* is gently wheeled out intact through the now-permeable membrane.

The locksmith's *speed of opening* depends on:
- How many locksmiths you hire (enzyme concentration)
- The ambient temperature (reaction kinetics, $T$)
- The pH of the workshop (buffer conditions, optimum pH)
- Whether there are co-factors (e.g., EDTA destabilizes outer membrane by chelating Mg²⁺/Ca²⁺)

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Definition | Typical Range / Value |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Lysis efficiency ($\eta_L$)** | Fraction of intracellular product released into bulk | $0.70$–$0.98$ |
| **Specific enzyme loading ($E_s$)** | Units of enzyme per gram dry cell weight (DCW) | $50$–$500 \text{ U/g DCW}$ |
| **Wall digestion time ($t_d$)** | Time required for $\geq 95\%$ envelope permeabilization | $15$–$120 \text{ min}$ |
| **Optical density ratio ($OD_{600}$)** | Turbidity proxy for unbroken cells | $0.1$–$1.5$ |
| **Michaelis constant ($K_m$) for lysozyme on *E. coli* peptidoglycan** | Substrate concentration at half-max velocity | $\approx 0.05 \text{ mg/mL}$ |
| **Turnover number ($k_{cat}$) for lysozyme** | Maximum substrate molecules converted per enzyme per second | $\approx 0.5 \text{ s}^{-1}$ |
| **Operating temperature ($T_{opt}$)** | Optimal lysis temperature | $30$–$37\text{ °C}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded metrics** above are *standard engineering values* used in KTU numerical problems and are NOT to be confused with literature-specific species/strain data.

### 1.4 Classification of Cell Walls (Why Enzymes Vary)

$$\text{Gram-positive} \xrightarrow{\text{thick PG}} \text{Lysozyme + EDTA + Osmotic shock}$$

$$\text{Gram-negative} \xrightarrow{\text{outer membrane + thin PG}} \text{Lysozyme + Triton X-100 + EDTA + Osmotic shock}$$

$$\text{Yeast} \xrightarrow{\text{β-glucan + mannoprotein}} \text{Zymolyase / Lyticase / Glucanase + Proteinase K}$$

$$\text{Plant cells} \xrightarrow{\text{cellulose + hemicellulose + pectin}} \text{Cellulase + Macerozyme + Pectinase}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks *"Justify the enzyme cocktail chosen for a given organism."* Always cite the **specific bond cleaved** (e.g., lysozyme cleaves the β-1,4-glycosidic bond between NAG and NAM).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Mechanistic Theory of Enzymatic Lysis

Enzymatic cell lysis is a **heterogeneous, pseudo-bimolecular reaction** where:

$$\underbrace{E}_{\text{enzyme in bulk}} + \underbrace{S_{\text{wall}}}_{\text{substrate in solid wall}} \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} \underbrace{ES}_{\text{complex on wall}} \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} \underbrace{E}_{\text{free}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{hydrolysate}}$$

The process is intrinsically **mass-transfer limited + kinetically limited**. A rigorous model must therefore incorporate:

1. **External mass transfer** — diffusion of enzyme from bulk to cell surface.
2. **Internal mass transfer / wall penetration** — diffusion within the porous peptidoglycan mesh.
3. **Surface enzymatic reaction** — Michaelis–Menten kinetics.
4. **Product release** — back-diffusion of intracellular components.

### 2.2 The Three Governing Rate Regimes

| Regime | Rate-Limiting Step | Engineering Implication |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **I — Reaction-limited** | $k_{cat} \ll k_L a$ | Increase enzyme dose, $T$, optimize pH |
| **II — External diffusion-limited** | $k_L a \ll k_{cat}$ | Increase agitation (impeller $N$, tip speed $v_t$) |
| **III — Internal diffusion-limited** | $D_e \ll k_{cat}$ | Reduce cell aggregate size, increase enzyme access |

Where:
- $k_L a$ = volumetric liquid-side mass transfer coefficient ($\text{s}^{-1}$)
- $D_e$ = effective diffusivity inside the wall ($\text{m}^2\text{/s}$)
- $v_t = \pi N D_i$ = impeller tip speed (m/s)

### 2.3 First-Order Disruption Model (Most Used in KTU Problems)

The fractional disruption $D_f$ follows:

$$\boxed{D_f(t) = 1 - e^{-k_d t}}$$

where $k_d$ is the pseudo-first-order disruption rate constant ($\text{min}^{-1}$).

The corresponding intracellular product concentration released is:

$$C_P(t) = C_P^{\infty} \cdot D_f(t) = C_P^{\infty}\,(1 - e^{-k_d t})$$

Derivation rationale:
- Cells behave as discrete "particles" each susceptible to enzymatic attack.
- Probability of survival is $e^{-k_d t}$ (Poisson process).
- Product release rate: $\dfrac{dC_P}{dt} = k_d\,(C_P^{\infty} - C_P)$

### 2.4 Michaelis–Menten Coupling with Wall Digestion

When enzyme concentration is in the *same order* as effective wall substrate, we replace first-order with Michaelis–Menten:

$$\boxed{v_{\text{lysis}} = \frac{v_{\max}\, C_E}{K_m + C_E} = k_{cat}\,C_{E,0}\,\frac{C_S}{K_m' + C_S}}$$

where:
- $C_E$ = enzyme concentration in bulk (U/mL or mg/mL)
- $C_S$ = effective wall substrate concentration (mg/mL)
- $K_m'$ = apparent $K_m$ accounting for mass-transfer resistance
- $v_{\max} = k_{cat}\,C_{E,0}$ = maximum lysis velocity

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Variable | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D_f(t) = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$ | Fractional cell disruption (first order) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_P(t) = C_P^{\infty}(1 - e^{-k_d t})$ | Released product concentration | g/L or mg/mL |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dC_P}{dt} = k_d(C_P^{\infty} - C_P)$ | Differential form | g/(L·min) |
| 4 | $v_{\text{lysis}} = \dfrac{v_{\max}C_E}{K_m + C_E}$ | Michaelis–Menten lysis velocity | g/(L·min) |
| 5 | $K_m' = K_m\!\left(1 + \dfrac{k_{cat}}{k_L a}\right)$ | Apparent $K_m$ with external resistance | g/L |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{int}}}{\eta_{\text{int}} + \text{Bi}_M}$ | Effectiveness factor concept (Thiele modulus analog) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Bi}_M = \dfrac{k_{cat}\,L}{D_e}$ | Biot number for mass transfer (lysis analog) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\phi^2 = \dfrac{k_{cat}\,L^2}{D_e\,K_m'}$ | Thiele modulus squared | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\phi\,h = \dfrac{\tanh\phi}{\phi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \phi^2/\text{Bi}_M}$ | Internal effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{O_2} = k_L a(C^* - C)$ | Oxygen transfer (only relevant for aerobic pre-lysis growth) | mmol/(L·h) |
| 11 | $\text{Sp. release} = \dfrac{C_P^{\infty}}{X_0}$ | Product released per unit biomass | mg/g DCW |
| 12 | $\tau_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Mean residence time (CSTR design) | min or h |
| 13 | $\text{Volumetric productivity} = \dfrac{C_P^{\infty} - C_P^{\text{in}}}{\tau_{\text{res}}}$ | Reactor productivity | mg/(L·h) |
| 14 | $E_s = \dfrac{U_{\text{enzyme}}}{X_0}$ | Specific enzyme loading | U/g DCW |
| 15 | $\tau_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{3}{k_d}$ | Optimal lysis time (for $>95\%$ release) | min |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never** use the pipe character $\vert$ inside math. Use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $K_m \mid$ would break the table renderer).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Enzymatic lysis is the *preferred* upstream disruption in:
- **Recombinant protein recovery** from *E. coli* — preserves fragile inclusion-body proteins.
- **Plasmid DNA isolation** for gene therapy vectors — minimal genomic-DNA shearing.
- **Viral vaccine manufacture** — intact viral capsids released without antigenic denaturation.
- **Intracellular metabolite recovery** — secondary metabolites, pigments (e.g., astaxanthin from *Haematococcus*).

The trade-off is **cost & time**: enzymes add ₹₹ to COG (cost of goods) and require 30–120 min vs. 5 min for mechanical lysis. Justification comes from **product quality** (activity retention $>95\%$ vs. $60\text{–}80\%$ for French press).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation I — First-Order Disruption Model

**Step 1 — Define the disruption fraction:**
$$D_f = \frac{X_0 - X(t)}{X_0} = 1 - \frac{X(t)}{X_0}$$

where $X_0$ = initial intact-cell concentration (g DCW/L), $X(t)$ = intact cells remaining at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Assume first-order decay of intact cells:**
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -k_d\,X(t)$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:**
$$\int_{X_0}^{X(t)} \frac{dX}{X} = -k_d \int_0^t dt \;\Rightarrow\; \ln\frac{X(t)}{X_0} = -k_d t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X(t)$ and substitute into $D_f$:**
$$X(t) = X_0\,e^{-k_d t} \;\Rightarrow\; D_f(t) = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$$

**Step 5 — Product release rate** (assuming product is released proportionally to cells lysed):
$$\frac{dC_P}{dt} = k_d\,X_0\,P_{\text{cell}} \cdot e^{-k_d t} = k_d\,(C_P^{\infty} - C_P)$$

where $P_{\text{cell}}$ = product content per cell (mg/g DCW), and $C_P^{\infty} = X_0\,P_{\text{cell}}$.

**Step 6 — Integrate for $C_P(t)$:**
$$C_P(t) = C_P^{\infty}\,(1 - e^{-k_d t})$$

**Step 7 — Time for 99% release:**
$$0.99 = 1 - e^{-k_d t_{99}} \;\Rightarrow\; t_{99} = \frac{\ln(100)}{k_d} = \frac{4.605}{k_d}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Michaelis–Menten Apparent $K_m$ with External Mass-Transfer Resistance

**Step 1 — Steady-state at the cell surface** (enzyme bulk $C_E$ → film → surface $C_{E,s}$):
$$\text{Flux to surface} = \text{Surface reaction rate}$$

$$k_L\,(C_E - C_{E,s}) = \frac{k_{cat}\,C_{E,s}\,C_{S,s}}{K_m + C_{S,s}}$$

**Step 2 — For excess wall substrate ($C_{S,s} \gg K_m$):**

$$k_L(C_E - C_{E,s}) = k_{cat}\,C_{E,s} \;\Rightarrow\; C_{E,s} = \frac{k_L\,C_E}{k_{cat} + k_L}$$

**Step 3 — Observed rate** (using bulk $C_E$):

$$v_{\text{obs}} = \frac{k_{cat}\,C_{E,s}}{K_m} = \frac{k_{cat}}{K_m}\cdot\frac{k_L\,C_E}{k_{cat} + k_L}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to M–M form:**
$$v_{\text{obs}} = \frac{v_{\max}\,C_E}{K_m\!\left(1 + \dfrac{k_{cat}}{k_L}\right) + C_E}$$

**Step 5 — Define apparent constant:**
$$\boxed{K_m' = K_m\!\left(1 + \frac{k_{cat}}{k_L}\right) = K_m\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\text{Bi}_M}\right)}$$

This shows external mass transfer *raises* $K_m$, *lowering* observed affinity.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Effectiveness Factor for Internal Diffusion in Cell Wall

**Step 1 — Steady-state enzyme balance in a 1-D wall slab of thickness $L$:**

$$D_e\,\frac{d^2 C_E}{dz^2} = \frac{k_{cat}\,C_E}{K_m + C_E}$$

**Step 2 — Non-dimensionalize** with $\zeta = z/L$, $u = C_E/C_{E,s}$:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{d\zeta^2} = \phi^2\,\frac{u}{1 + \beta u}$$

where $\phi^2 = k_{cat}L^2/(D_e K_m)$, $\beta = C_{E,s}/K_m$.

**Step 3 — For first-order limit ($C_{E,s} \ll K_m$):**

$$\frac{d^2 u}{d\zeta^2} = \phi^2 u \;\Rightarrow\; u = \frac{\cosh[\phi(1-\zeta)]}{\cosh\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Effectiveness factor:**
$$\eta = \frac{-D_e\left.\dfrac{dC_E}{dz}\right|_{z=L}}{k_{cat}\,C_{E,s}/K_m\cdot L} = \frac{\tanh\phi}{\phi}$$

**Step 5 — With external resistance (combined):**

$$\eta_{\text{total}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{internal}}}{1 + \dfrac{\phi^2}{\text{Bi}_M\,\eta_{\text{internal}}}}$$

**Engineering takeaway:** small cell aggregates, vigorous mixing, high $D_e$ (achieved via hypo-osmotic pre-treatment) all push $\eta \to 1$ and maximize lysis efficiency.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Lysis Rate | Product Quality | Cost | Scalability | Shear Sensitivity | Best Application |
|--------|------------|------------------|------|-------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Lysozyme (Gram –)** | Moderate | Excellent | Medium-High | Excellent | None | Recombinant protein |
| **Zymolyase (Yeast)** | Moderate | Excellent | Medium | Excellent | None | Plasmid/intracellular enzyme |
| **Proteinase K (Universal)** | Fast | Variable (digests target proteins!) | Medium | Excellent | None | DNA extraction only |
| **Multi-enzyme cocktail** | Fast | Excellent | High | Excellent | None | Vaccine, fragile targets |
| **Bead milling (compared)** | Very Fast | Good | Low | Excellent | High | Bulk intracellular products |
| **French press (compared)** | Fast | Good | Medium | Poor | Moderate | Lab-scale only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Enzymatic Lysis Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell Biomass X0 g DCW per L] --> B[Cell Washing and Resuspension Buffer pH 7.5]
    B --> C{Pre-treatment}
    C -->|Chelation| D[EDTA 1 mM disrupts outer membrane]
    C -->|Osmotic shock| E[10 mM Tris 0.5 M sucrose]
    C -->|Surfactant| F[Triton X 100 0.1 percent v per v]
    D --> G[Enzyme Addition Lysozyme 50 to 500 U per g DCW]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Incubation 30 to 37 deg C 15 to 120 min gentle agitation 100 rpm]
    H --> I[Wall Hydrolysis Beta 1 4 glycosidic bonds cleaved]
    I --> J[Membrane Permeabilization]
    J --> K[Intracellular Product Release Diffusion governed]
    K --> L[Lysate Clarification Centrifugation or Microfiltration]
    L --> M[Further Downstream Chromatography Capture]
    H --> N[Inline OD600 Monitoring Turbidity Drop]
    N --> O{Reached 95 percent disruption}
    O -->|Yes| P[Stop Reaction Add Protease Inhibitor PMSF 1 mM]
    O -->|No| H
    P --> L
```

### 4.2 Reactor Configuration Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start Process Specification] --> T1{Batch or Continuous}
    T1 -->|High value Low volume| P1[Batch Stirred Tank Reactor BSTR]
    T1 -->|Large scale Steady state| P2[CSTR Continuous]
    P1 --> Q1[Single enzyme dose Long residence time]
    P2 --> Q2[Enzyme feed stream Steady disruption profile]
    Q1 --> R1[Advantages Flexibility Easy validation GMP friendly]
    Q2 --> R2[Advantages Productivity Smaller footprint]
    R1 --> END[Downstream Processing]
    R2 --> END
```

### 4.3 Mass-Transfer Cascade Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Enzyme CE bulk] -->|External film kL| B[Wall Surface CE s]
    B -->|Internal diffusion De| C[Inner Wall CE inner]
    C -->|Surface reaction kcat| D[Wall Hydrolysate]
    D -->|Back diffusion| E[Bulk Product CP]
    E --> F[Clarification]
```

### 4.4 Multi-Enzyme Cocktail Strategy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Organism] --> B{Gram status}
    B -->|Gram negative| C[Outer membrane compromise]
    B -->|Gram positive| D[Direct PG attack]
    B -->|Yeast| E[Glucan plus Mannoprotein]
    B -->|Plant| F[Cellulose plus Pectin]
    C --> C1[EDTA plus Triton]
    C1 --> C2[Lysozyme cleaves PG]
    D --> D1[Lysozyme primary]
    D1 --> D2[Optional Proteinase K for protein release]
    E --> E1[Zymolyase or Glucanase]
    E1 --> E2[Proteinase K or Chitinase]
    F --> F1[Cellulase plus Macerozyme]
    F1 --> F2[Pectinase]
    C2 --> G[Combined Lysate]
    D2 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F2 --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between mechanical and enzymatic cell lysis with three key criteria.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~100 words):**

| Criterion | Mechanical Lysis | Enzymatic Lysis |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Principle** | Physical disruption (shear, cavitation, impact) | Biochemical hydrolysis of cell-wall bonds |
| **Selectivity** | Non-selective; damages all components | Highly selective; specific bond cleavage |
| **Product quality** | Moderate (denaturation possible) | Excellent (gentle, ambient T) |
| **Scale-up** | Easy (bead mill, homogenizer) | Easy (chemical reactor) |
| **Cost per kg biomass** | Lower | Higher (enzyme cost) |
| **Typical organisms** | Robust bacteria, yeast | Gram-veg, fragile recombinant cells |

*(1 mark for principle, 1 mark for selectivity, 1 mark for product-quality comparison)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain why lysozyme alone is insufficient for Gram-negative bacteria and what co-factors are required.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lysozyme cleaves the β-1,4-glycosidic bond between N-acetylglucosamine (NAG) and N-acetylmuramic acid (NAM) in **peptidoglycan (PG)**. In Gram-negative bacteria, the PG layer is **shielded by an outer membrane (OM)** containing LPS and stabilized by Mg²⁺/Ca²⁺ divalent cation bridges. Without destabilizing the OM, lysozyme cannot access its substrate.

**Required co-factors (1 mark each):**
1. **EDTA (1 mM)** — chelates Mg²⁺/Ca²⁺, releases LPS, permeabilizes OM.
2. **Triton X-100 (0.1% v/v)** — solubilizes outer-membrane lipids.
3. **Osmotic stabilizer (sucrose 0.5 M)** — prevents premature spheroplast lysis.
4. **Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5–8.0)** — optimum for lysozyme activity.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the first-order disruption model $D_f(t) = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$ for enzymatic cell lysis and obtain the expression for the time required to achieve 99% product release. **7 marks**

**(b)** A recombinant *E. coli* fermentation yields a biomass of $X_0 = 8 \text{ g DCW/L}$ with intracellular product content $P_{\text{cell}} = 60 \text{ mg/g DCW}$. Lysozyme is added at $E_s = 200 \text{ U/g DCW}$ and the measured first-order disruption constant is $k_d = 0.08 \text{ min}^{-1}$ at $37\text{ °C}$. Calculate:
   (i) Maximum releasable product concentration $C_P^{\infty}$.
   (ii) Product concentration after $30$ min.
   (iii) Time for 95% release.
   (iv) Specific enzyme loading required if 95% release must be achieved in exactly 20 min. **7 marks**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from cell balance (1 mark):
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -k_d\,X(t)$$

Separation and integration (2 marks):
$$\int_{X_0}^{X} \frac{dX}{X} = -k_d \int_0^t dt \;\Rightarrow\; X(t) = X_0\,e^{-k_d t}$$

Disruption fraction (1 mark):
$$D_f(t) = 1 - \frac{X(t)}{X_0} = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$$

Product release (1 mark):
$$C_P(t) = C_P^{\infty}\,(1 - e^{-k_d t})$$

99% time derivation (2 marks):
$$0.99 = 1 - e^{-k_d t_{99}} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-k_d t_{99}} = 0.01 \;\Rightarrow\; t_{99} = \frac{\ln 100}{k_d} = \frac{4.605}{k_d}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i)** $C_P^{\infty} = X_0 \cdot P_{\text{cell}} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ mg/L}$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii)** $C_P(30) = 480\,(1 - e^{-0.08 \times 30}) = 480\,(1 - e^{-2.4}) = 480\,(1 - 0.0907) = 480 \times 0.9093 = 436.5 \text{ mg/L}$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** $t_{95} = \dfrac{\ln(20)}{k_d} = \dfrac{\ln 20}{0.08} = \dfrac{2.996}{0.08} = 37.45 \text{ min}$ **(1 mark)**

**(iv)** Required new $k_d' = \dfrac{\ln 20}{20} = \dfrac{2.996}{20} = 0.1498 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Assuming $k_d \propto E_s$ (linear regime): $E_s' = 200 \times \dfrac{0.1498}{0.08} = 374.5 \text{ U/g DCW}$ **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the three regimes of enzymatic lysis (reaction-limited, external-diffusion limited, internal-diffusion limited) with appropriate mathematical criteria. **7 marks**

**(b)** A yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) culture with $X_0 = 12 \text{ g DCW/L}$ is treated with zymolyase ($E_s = 300 \text{ U/g DCW}$) at $30\text{ °C}$, pH 7.5. The Michaelis–Menten parameters are $K_m = 0.04 \text{ mg/mL}$, $v_{\max} = 0.85 \text{ mg/(mL·min)}$, and external mass-transfer coefficient $k_L a = 0.5 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Calculate:
   (i) Apparent $K_m'$ accounting for external resistance.
   (ii) Observed lysis rate at $C_E = 0.08 \text{ mg/mL}$.
   (iii) Reactor volume for $V = Q \cdot \tau$ if $Q = 50 \text{ L/h}$ and $\tau = 90 \text{ min}$.
   (iv) Comment on whether this is reaction-limited or mass-transfer-limited. **7 marks**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Discussion (7 marks):**

- **Regime I — Reaction-limited** (2 marks): Occurs when $k_{cat}/K_m \ll k_L a / C_E$. Criterion: $\phi^2 \ll 1$ and $\text{Bi}_M \gg 1$. Engineering fix: increase $T$, $C_E$, optimize pH.
- **Regime II — External-diffusion limited** (2 marks): When $k_L a \ll k_{cat} \cdot C_E / (K_m + C_E)$. Criterion: $\text{Bi}_M \ll 1$. Engineering fix: increase agitation, gas-liquid interfacial area (baffles).
- **Regime III — Internal-diffusion limited** (2 marks): When $D_e / L^2 \ll k_{cat}/K_m$. Criterion: $\phi^2 \gg 1$. Engineering fix: pre-treat cells, reduce aggregate size.
- Diagnostic (1 mark): Damköhler number $\text{Da} = k_{cat}/(k_L a)$ determines regime.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i)** $k_{cat} = v_{\max}/C_{E,\text{ref}}$. Assuming $C_E \approx v_{\max}/k_{cat} = 1 \text{ mg/mL} \Rightarrow k_{cat} = 0.85 \text{ min}^{-1}$.
$$K_m' = K_m\!\left(1 + \frac{k_{cat}}{k_L a}\right) = 0.04\!\left(1 + \frac{0.85}{0.5}\right) = 0.04 \times 2.7 = 0.108 \text{ mg/mL}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii)** 
$$v_{\text{obs}} = \frac{v_{\max}\,C_E}{K_m' + C_E} = \frac{0.85 \times 0.08}{0.108 + 0.08} = \frac{0.068}{0.188} = 0.362 \text{ mg/(mL·min)}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** $V = Q \cdot \tau = 50 \text{ L/h} \times \dfrac{90}{60} \text{ h} = 75 \text{ L}$ **(1.5 marks)**

**(iv)** $\text{Da} = k_{cat}/(k_L a) = 0.85/0.5 = 1.7 > 1$ ⟹ **Reaction-limited regime dominates**. Recommendation: increase enzyme dose or temperature. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake #1 (loses 2 marks):** Writing $D_f(t) = e^{-k_d t}$ instead of $1 - e^{-k_d t}$. Remember: disruption = fraction *broken*, not fraction *surviving*.
> 
> **Common mistake #2 (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting units in $C_P$ or $k_d$. Always state $\text{mg/L}$ and $\text{min}^{-1}$ explicitly.
> 
> **Common mistake #3 (loses 2 marks):** Confusing $C_E$ (enzyme in bulk) with $C_{E,s}$ (enzyme at wall surface). Use the apparent $K_m'$ when external resistance is non-negligible.
> 
> **Common mistake #4 (loses 3 marks):** Failing to specify co-factors (EDTA, Triton, osmotic stabilizer) when justifying enzyme choice for Gram-negative cells — partial credit at best.
> 
> **Common mistake #5 (loses 2 marks):** Choosing *mechanical lysis* in a question asking specifically about *enzymatic* advantages — the question setter expects enzymatic solution with co-factor justification.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Enzymatic lysis = selective, biocatalytic, mass-transfer + kinetic limited.**
- **First-order model** $D_f = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$ is the workhorse; valid when $C_E \ll K_m$.
- **Michaelis–Menten** applies when enzyme dose is comparable to apparent $K_m$.
- **Apparent $K_m' = K_m(1 + k_{cat}/k_L a)$** captures external diffusion penalty.
- **Thiele modulus** $\phi^2 = k_{cat}L^2/(D_e K_m)$ captures internal diffusion.
- **$t_{95} = \ln(20)/k_d \approx 3/k_d$** — quick rule-of-thumb for design.
- **$t_{99} = \ln(100)/k_d \approx 4.6/k_d$** — for regulatory batch-release specs.
- **Specific enzyme loading** $E_s = 50\text{–}500 \text{ U/g DCW}$ — design starting point.
- **Co-factors for Gram-negative:** EDTA + Triton + osmotic stabilizer + Tris buffer.
- **Co-factors for yeast:** Zymolyase + Proteinase K + sorbitol osmotic stabilizer.
- **Damköhler number $\text{Da} = k_{cat}/(k_L a)$** is the regime-selection diagnostic.
- **Product quality advantage:** $>95\%$ activity retention vs. $60\text{–}80\%$ for mechanical.
- **Cost penalty:** ₹₹ from enzyme dosing — justified only when product value is high.
- **Reactor design choice:** BSTR for flexibility & GMP; CSTR for scale & steady-state.
- **Optimal $\tau \approx 3/k_d$** for $>95\%$ release at minimum enzyme dose.
- **Always cite the bond cleaved** (β-1,4-glycosidic for lysozyme; β-1,3-glucan for zymolyase; peptide bonds for proteinase K).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Systems-Process Description, Theory of Dialysis, Reverse Osmosis, Ultrafiltration and Crossflow Filtration, Batch Continuous and Diafiltration Modes, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT865 - ADVANCED BIO SEPARATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Membrane Systems-Process Description, Theory of Dialysis, Reverse Osmosis, Ultrafiltration and Crossflow Filtration, Batch Continuous and Diafiltration Modes, Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Membrane-Based Separations: Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Membrane Separation** is a unit operation in which a **selectively permeable barrier (membrane)** allows certain components of a feed mixture to pass through it while rejecting others, driven by a **thermodynamic potential gradient** (pressure, concentration, temperature, or electrical potential). The selectivity and the magnitude of the driving force determine the separation efficiency.

In the context of **Advanced Bio-Separation Engineering (PEBBT865)**, membrane systems constitute a **rate-controlled (kinetically controlled)** separation process — unlike equilibrium-based processes such as extraction or distillation. The membrane itself does not reach thermodynamic equilibrium with either the upstream (retentate) or downstream (permeate) stream; instead, the **rate of mass transfer** through the membrane determines both the productivity and the purity of separation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why membrane separations dominate downstream bioprocessing:**
> - Operate at **ambient temperature** (gentle on thermolabile biomolecules like enzymes, antibodies, vaccines).
> - **No phase change** — energy consumption is largely mechanical (pumping), not thermal.
> - Scalable from **laboratory (mL scale) → industrial (m³ scale)** without changing the principle.
> - Compatible with **continuous** and **single-use** operation, key for modern biopharma.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sieve-Coffee-Filter Story

Imagine you have a jar of muddy river water and you pour it through a **fine cloth**. The water drips through, but the **mud particles** stay behind. That is essentially what a membrane does — only the engineering version is vastly more sophisticated.

- **Cloth pores** → Membrane pores (with precisely engineered sizes from **1 nm** in Reverse Osmosis to **10 µm** in Microfiltration).
- **Gravity** (the simple driver) → In membrane engineering, the driver is a precisely controlled **pressure gradient (ΔP)**, **concentration gradient (Δc)**, or **electrical potential gradient (ΔE)**.
- **Dirt (retained material)** → The **retentate** stream.
- **Clean water (passed material)** → The **permeate** stream.

Now picture doing this with a **counter-current river flow** sweeping over the cloth so the cloth does not clog. That sweeping flow is what engineers call **crossflow filtration** — and it is the single most important practical innovation that allowed membrane processes to become industrial workhorses.

---

### 1.3 Spectrum of Pressure-Driven Membrane Processes

Membrane processes are most commonly classified by the **magnitude of the applied pressure** and the **size of the species being separated**. The table below summarizes the key processes relevant to Module 2.

| Process | Driving Force | Typical ΔP (bar) | Membrane Pore Size | Species Rejected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | Pressure | **0.1 – 2** | 0.1 – 10 µm | Cells, suspended solids, bacteria |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | Pressure | **1 – 10** | 1 – 100 nm | Proteins, viruses, macromolecules |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | Pressure | **10 – 40** | 0.5 – 5 nm | Multivalent ions, small organics |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | Pressure | **15 – 80** | < 0.5 nm (dense) | Monovalent ions, water purification |
| **Dialysis** | Concentration | ≈ 1 (osmotic) | 1 – 10 nm | Small solutes (pass) vs macromolecules (retain) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Membrane Performance Metric:** The two cardinal numbers that completely characterize a membrane are:
> 1. **Permeate Flux, J** (units: **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹** or **LMH**) — the volumetric rate of permeate per unit membrane area.
> 2. **Rejection Coefficient, R (dimensionless)** — a number between **0** (no rejection) and **1** (complete rejection) defined below.

---

### 1.4 Process Description — Generic Membrane Unit

Every membrane separation — whether it is dialysis, ultrafiltration, or reverse osmosis — has the same **four functional elements**:

1. **Feed (F)** — the inlet solution containing the target solute(s) and contaminants.
2. **Membrane Module** — the heart of the system, where the membrane is housed (configurations include **flat sheet, spiral wound, hollow fiber, tubular**).
3. **Retentate (R)** — the stream enriched in the species that **do NOT pass** the membrane (also called the "concentrate" or "hold-up").
4. **Permeate (P)** — the stream that **passes through** the membrane (also called the "filtrate").

A **steady-state mass balance** on any solute gives:

$$F \cdot c_F \;=\; R \cdot c_R \;+\; P \cdot c_P$$

and on the solvent (typically water):

$$F \;=\; R \;+\; P$$

These two balances will appear repeatedly throughout derivations in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theory of Dialysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dialysis** is a **concentration-gradient-driven** membrane separation in which **small solute molecules** diffuse through a porous membrane from a high-concentration side to a low-concentration side, while **large solute molecules** (macromolecules, colloids, proteins) are retained because they are too large to enter the membrane pores.

Dialysis is the **passive counterpart** to ultrafiltration: UF uses pressure to **push** solvent through; dialysis lets solute **diffuse** spontaneously down its own concentration gradient.

**Fick's First Law of Diffusion (the engine of dialysis):**

$$J_s \;=\; -D_s \cdot \frac{dc_s}{dx}$$

where:
- $J_s$ = solute flux through the membrane (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $D_s$ = effective solute diffusivity within the membrane pores (m²·s⁻¹)
- $\dfrac{dc_s}{dx}$ = solute concentration gradient across the membrane (mol·m⁻⁴)

Across a membrane of thickness $\delta_m$, integrating Fick's law gives the **dialysis flux equation**:

$$J_s \;=\; \frac{D_s \cdot \Delta c_s}{\delta_m}$$

where $\Delta c_s$ is the difference in solute concentration between the retentate (dialysate feed) and the permeate (dialysate) sides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two critical limitations of dialysis** (and why it is not used for industrial bulk separations):
> 1. **Extremely slow** — driven only by molecular diffusion, which is orders of magnitude slower than pressure-driven flux.
> 2. **No solvent removal** — dialysis cannot concentrate the retentate; it can only **exchange solutes**.
>
> Despite this, dialysis remains the **gold-standard** technique in biochemistry labs for **desalting proteins** and **removing unreacted small-molecule reagents**.

---

### 2.2 Theory of Reverse Osmosis (RO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Osmosis** is a **pressure-driven** membrane process in which a hydraulic pressure **greater than the osmotic pressure difference** between the feed and the permeate is applied to **reverse the natural osmotic flow**, thereby forcing the solvent (usually water) from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration.

**Osmotic Pressure (van 't Hoff equation for ideal dilute solutions):**

$$\pi \;=\; i \cdot c \cdot R \cdot T$$

where:
- $\pi$ = osmotic pressure (Pa)
- $i$ = van 't Hoff dissociation factor (dimensionless; $i = 1$ for non-electrolytes, $i = 2$ for NaCl, etc.)
- $c$ = molar solute concentration (mol·m⁻³)
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Driving Force for Solvent Flux in RO (the net effective pressure):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \Delta P_{\text{applied}} \;-\; \Delta \pi$$

Solvent (water) flux in RO is given by the **solution-diffusion model**:

$$J_w \;=\; A \cdot (\Delta P_{\text{applied}} - \Delta \pi)$$

where $A$ is the **pure water permeability constant** (m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) — a property of the specific RO membrane.

Solute flux in RO is **independent** of pressure and governed by:

$$J_s \;=\; B \cdot \Delta c_s$$

where $B$ is the **solute permeability constant** (m·s⁻¹).

**Rejection Coefficient (true, observed, and intrinsic):**

$$R_{\text{obs}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{c_P}{c_F} \quad ; \quad R_{\text{true}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{c_P}{c_M}$$

where $c_M$ is the solute concentration **at the membrane surface** (NOT in the bulk feed) — this distinction is what makes concentration polarization so important.

---

### 2.3 Theory of Ultrafiltration (UF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrafiltration** is a **pressure-driven** membrane process that separates macromolecules (proteins, polymers, colloids) from solvents and small solutes using membranes with pore sizes in the range **1 – 100 nm**. The applied pressure is typically **1 – 10 bar**, much lower than RO because UF membranes are porous (not dense).

**Pure water flux through a UF membrane (Darcy's law analog):**

$$J \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

where:
- $J$ = volumetric permeate flux (m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa)
- $\mu$ = viscosity of the permeate (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance (m⁻¹)

**Hagen-Poiseuille equation for flow through cylindrical pores** (gives a molecular interpretation of $R_m$):

$$R_m \;=\; \frac{8 \cdot \delta_m}{N \cdot \pi \cdot r_p^{4}}$$

where:
- $\delta_m$ = membrane thickness
- $N$ = number of pores per unit area
- $r_p$ = effective pore radius

> [!NOTE]
> This shows why **small pore-size variations** have a dramatic effect on flux — flux scales as $r_p^4$. Halving the pore radius reduces flux by a factor of **16**.

**Resistance-in-Series Model for Fouled Membranes:**

$$J \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_c)}$$

where $R_f$ is the **fouling resistance** (irreversible pore plugging) and $R_c$ is the **cake layer resistance** (deposited solute layer, often reversible by crossflow).

**Gel-Polarization Model (the limiting-flux law of UF):**

$$J \;=\; k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{c_g}{c_b}\right)$$

where:
- $k$ = mass-transfer coefficient in the boundary layer (m·s⁻¹)
- $c_g$ = gel concentration (a constant for a given solute–membrane pair)
- $c_b$ = bulk solute concentration

This is the **most tested equation** in KTU UF problems.

---

### 2.4 Crossflow Filtration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossflow Filtration** (also called **tangential-flow filtration, TFF**) is a mode of operation in which the feed stream flows **parallel to the membrane surface**, sweeping away deposited particles and reducing the **cake layer** that would otherwise build up in dead-end operation.

The flow geometry has two velocity components:
- **Crossflow velocity** ($u_c$) — parallel to membrane, produces shear that detaches deposits.
- **Permeate velocity** ($v_w$) — perpendicular to membrane, equals $J$ (the permeate flux).

The dimensionless **wall shear rate** governs back-transport:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w \;=\; \frac{8 \cdot u_c}{d_h}$$

where $d_h$ is the hydraulic diameter of the flow channel.

The **mass-transfer coefficient** in a crossflow channel is correlated by the **Sherwood number**:

$$\text{Sh} \;=\; \frac{k \cdot d_h}{D} \;=\; a \cdot \text{Re}^{b} \cdot \text{Sc}^{c}$$

with typical values $a = 0.023$, $b = 0.875$, $c = 0.25$ (laminar channel) for the **Dériván–Gekas correlation**, and Reynolds number:

$$\text{Re} \;=\; \frac{\rho \cdot u_c \cdot d_h}{\mu}$$

**Back-Transport Mechanisms (the "self-cleaning" of crossflow):**

| Mechanism | Best for | Back-flux scaling |
|---|---|---|
| Brownian diffusion | Small colloids (< 0.1 µm) | $J \propto \ln(c_g/c_b)$ — gel model |
| Shear-induced diffusion | Larger particles (0.5 – 50 µm) | $J \propto \dot{\gamma}_w^{0.33}$ |
| Inertial lift | Very large particles (> 50 µm) | $J \propto \dot{\gamma}_w^{1.5}$ |

---

### 2.5 Batch, Continuous, and Diafiltration Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diafiltration** is a specialized membrane operating mode in which **fresh buffer (water or solvent) is added to the retentate at the same rate as permeate is removed**, thereby maintaining a **constant retentate volume** while **washing out small permeable solutes** (salts, low-MW contaminants). It is the membrane analog of a **multi-stage washing** operation.

**Three principal operating modes for membrane systems:**

1. **Batch (Closed-loop) Concentration** — Feed is placed in a tank, permeate is withdrawn, retentate is recycled to the tank. The volume in the tank decreases continuously; the solute of interest becomes progressively concentrated. The instantaneous flux declines because $c_b$ rises and viscosity rises.

2. **Continuous (Single-pass / Open-loop)** — Feed enters at one end, retentate exits the other, permeate is withdrawn through the membrane. The system operates at **steady state**. A recycle loop may be added to boost crossflow velocity; this is called **feed-and-bleed**.

3. **Diafiltration (DF)** — Two sub-modes:
   - **Constant-Volume Diafiltration (CVDF):** Buffer is added at the same rate as permeate is withdrawn, so the retentate volume stays constant. Used to **remove permeable impurities** without further concentrating the target.
   - **Variable-Volume Diafiltration (VVDF):** Buffer is added without simultaneous permeate removal (or permeate is removed without buffer addition). Used when partial concentration AND partial desalting are both needed.

**Degree of Separation (η) for a Solute in Diafiltration:**

$$\eta \;=\; 1 - \exp(-N)$$

where $N$ is the **number of diafiltration volumes** (defined as the ratio of total added buffer volume to the retentate volume):

$$N \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{buffer}}}{V_{\text{retentate}}}$$

**Recovery / Yield (Y) for the Target Macromolecule:**

$$Y \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_p}{V_0}\right) \quad \text{when } \sigma_s \;=\; 1$$

where $\sigma_s$ is the **sieving coefficient** of the target. For an *imperfectly* rejected target with sieving coefficient $\sigma$, the recovery during concentration mode is:

$$Y \;=\; \left(\frac{V_f}{V_0}\right)^{\sigma - 1}$$

with $V_f$ = final retentate volume and $V_0$ = initial retentate volume.

---

### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Complete Reference)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Key Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass balance (solute) | $F c_F = R c_R + P c_P$ | kg·h⁻¹ or mol·h⁻¹ | Steady state |
| 2 | Osmotic pressure (van 't Hoff) | $\pi = i c R T$ | Pa, with $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Dilute ideal solution |
| 3 | Reverse osmosis flux | $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | $A$ in m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ |
| 4 | Reverse osmosis solute flux | $J_s = B \Delta c_s$ | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Pressure-independent |
| 5 | Observed rejection | $R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - c_P/c_F$ | dimensionless | $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 6 | UF permeate flux (clean membrane) | $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_m)$ | m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Darcy-type |
| 7 | UF Hagen-Poiseuille resistance | $R_m = 8 \delta_m / (N \pi r_p^4)$ | m⁻¹ | Cylindrical pore assumption |
| 8 | Gel-polarization limiting flux | $J = k \ln(c_g / c_b)$ | m·s⁻¹ | Macrosolute-limited |
| 9 | Resistance-in-series (fouling) | $J = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)]$ | m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | All resistances in series |
| 10 | Dialysis flux (Fick) | $J_s = D_s \Delta c_s / \delta_m$ | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Quiescent or thin-channel |
| 11 | Crossflow wall shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8 u_c / d_h$ | s⁻¹ | Laminar rectangular channel |
| 12 | Reynolds number | $\text{Re} = \rho u_c d_h / \mu$ | dimensionless | Turbulent if $\text{Re} > 2300$ |
| 13 | Sherwood number correlation | $\text{Sh} = 0.023\,\text{Re}^{0.875}\,\text{Sc}^{0.25}$ | dimensionless | Turbulent duct |
| 14 | Diafiltration separation | $\eta = 1 - \exp(-N)$ | dimensionless | $N$ = diavolumes |
| 15 | Yield during concentration | $Y = (V_f/V_0)^{\sigma - 1}$ | dimensionless | $V_f < V_0$ for concentration |
| 16 | Sieving coefficient | $\sigma = c_P / c_R$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \sigma \le 1$ |
| 17 | Membrane area requirement | $A_m = V_p / (J \cdot t)$ | m² | For batch sizing |
| 18 | Hydraulic diameter (rectangular) | $d_h = 4ab / (2(a+b))$ | m | $a$, $b$ = channel half-dimensions |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** In RO, students often forget that the osmotic pressure of the **concentrate side** (not the feed side) must be used in $\Delta\pi$. Use $c_R$, not $c_F$, in the van 't Hoff equation when the retentate and permeate are at very different compositions.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biopharmaceutical Industry:** UF/DF is the **workhorse for protein formulation** — monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are concentrated 10–20× and transferred into formulation buffer by combined UF + constant-volume diafiltration. This is the single most repeated unit operation in downstream bioprocessing.
- **Water Treatment:** RO produces potable water from seawater at **\lt 4 kWh·m⁻³** energy consumption, down from ~6 kWh·m⁻³ in the 1990s — modern plants (e.g., Sorek, Israel) treat **\gt 600,000 m³/day**.
- **Dairy Industry:** UF concentrates milk proteins to make cheese; whey proteins are recovered via UF as value-added ingredients.
- **Hemodialysis (Medical):** Hollow-fiber dialyzers clean blood of uremic toxins in kidney-failure patients — the same fundamental dialysis principle at a different scale.
- **Vaccine & Plasma Industry:** Crossflow UF removes ethanol from blood-plasma fractions at industrial scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Osmotic Pressure Difference in an RO System

**Problem:** A 0.05 mol·L⁻¹ NaCl solution at **25 °C** is processed by RO. The applied pressure is **30 bar**. The permeate is essentially pure water. Calculate the **net effective driving pressure** for water flux.

**Given:**
- $c = 0.05 \text{ mol·L}^{-1} = 50 \text{ mol·m}^{-3}$
- $T = 25\,°\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- $i = 2$ (NaCl fully dissociates)
- $\Delta P_{\text{applied}} = 30 \text{ bar} = 30 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\pi_{\text{permeate}} \approx 0$ (pure water)

**Step 1 — Convert concentration to SI units.**

$$c = 0.05 \, \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{L}} \times \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} = 50 \, \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the van 't Hoff equation.**

$$\pi = i \cdot c \cdot R \cdot T$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\pi = (2) \cdot (50 \, \text{mol·m}^{-3}) \cdot (8.314 \, \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}) \cdot (298.15 \, \text{K})$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the product step-by-step.**

$$\pi = 2 \times 50 \times 8.314 \times 298.15$$

First compute $2 \times 50 = 100$.

Then $100 \times 8.314 = 831.4$.

Then $831.4 \times 298.15$.

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$831.4 \times 298.15 = 831.4 \times (300 - 1.85) = 831.4 \times 300 - 831.4 \times 1.85$$

$$= 249420 - 1538.09 = 247881.91$$

**Step 4 — Express with appropriate units.**

$$\pi \approx 2.479 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 2.48 \, \text{bar}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net effective pressure.**

$$\Delta P_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P_{\text{applied}} - \Delta \pi = 30 - 2.48 = 27.52 \text{ bar}$$

**Conclusion:** Even though only **2.48 bar** of osmotic pressure opposes the flow, the practical rule of thumb in RO design is to apply a pressure **at least 10–15× the osmotic pressure** to maintain reasonable flux. So 30 bar applied against 2.48 bar osmotic gives a comfortable driving force of **27.5 bar**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for the KTU student:** Osmotic pressure is **colossally large** for seawater (~25 bar) and for concentrated sucrose solutions. This is why RO of seawater demands **\gt 60 bar** operating pressures and why RO membranes are made as **thin-film composites** — every micrometer of active-layer thickness reduces flux.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Membrane Area for a Batch UF Process

**Problem:** A 500 L fermentation broth containing a recombinant enzyme at **2 g·L⁻¹** is to be concentrated by UF to a final volume of **50 L** at **20 °C**. The membrane flux at the average bulk concentration is **20 LMH** (i.e., 20 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹). The flux is approximately constant because of adequate crossflow. Calculate the **membrane area** required and the **processing time**.

**Given:**
- $V_0 = 500 \text{ L}$ (initial volume)
- $V_f = 50 \text{ L}$ (final volume)
- $J = 20 \text{ LMH} = 20 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$
- Assumption: $J$ ≈ constant due to crossflow

**Step 1 — Compute the total permeate volume to be removed.**

$$V_p = V_0 - V_f = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the differential volume balance.**

In a batch concentration with constant flux, the permeate is withdrawn at a constant volumetric rate:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -J \cdot A_m$$

where $V$ is the retentate volume. Since $J$ is assumed constant:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $V_0$ to $V_f$.**

$$\int_{V_0}^{V_f} dV = -J \cdot A_m \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$V_f - V_0 = -J \cdot A_m \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{V_0 - V_f}{J \cdot A_m} = \frac{V_p}{J \cdot A_m}$$

**Step 4 — The processing time $t$ depends on the chosen membrane area. The area itself is the design choice, but a typical design criterion is to complete the batch in 5 hours.**

For $t_{\text{target}} = 5 \text{ h}$:

$$A_m = \frac{V_p}{J \cdot t_{\text{target}}} = \frac{450 \text{ L}}{20 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1} \times 5 \text{ h}}$$

$$A_m = \frac{450}{100} = 4.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check the concentration achieved.**

$$c_f = c_0 \cdot \frac{V_0}{V_f} = 2 \text{ g·L}^{-1} \times \frac{500}{50} = 20 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$$

So the enzyme is concentrated by a **volume concentration factor (VCF) of 10×**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important design insight:** For a constant-flux batch, $A_m$ is inversely proportional to $t_{\text{target}}$. To halve the processing time, you must double the membrane area — but doubling the area roughly doubles the capital cost. This is the classic **time-vs-area trade-off** in membrane-plant economics.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diafiltration Removal of an Impurity Salt

**Problem:** A 100 L UF retentate contains **0.5 M NaCl** and **10 g·L⁻¹ protein**. The protein is fully retained by the membrane ($\sigma_p = 0$, so $Y_p = 1$). The NaCl is freely permeable ($\sigma_s = 1$, so $\eta_{\text{NaCl}} = 1 - \exp(-N)$). How many **diavolumes** are required to reduce NaCl to **5 mM**? How much buffer must be added?

**Step 1 — Apply the diafiltration removal equation.**

$$\eta = 1 - \exp(-N) = \frac{c_0 - c_f}{c_0 - c_P^{(\infty)}}$$

In diafiltration with constant volume, the **residual solute fraction** is:

$$\frac{c_f}{c_0} = \exp(-N)$$

**Step 2 — Insert values.**

$$\frac{c_f}{c_0} = \exp(-N)$$

$$\frac{0.005 \text{ M}}{0.5 \text{ M}} = \exp(-N)$$

$$0.01 = \exp(-N)$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm.**

$$\ln(0.01) = -N$$

$$-4.6052 = -N$$

$$N = 4.61 \text{ diavolumes}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required buffer volume.**

$$N = \frac{V_{\text{buffer}}}{V_{\text{retentate}}} = \frac{V_{\text{buffer}}}{100 \text{ L}}$$

$$V_{\text{buffer}} = 4.61 \times 100 = 461 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5 — Verify by direct integration (optional but excellent for full marks).**

The differential equation for a perfectly mixed CVDF system is:

$$V \frac{dc}{dt} = -Q_P c$$

with $Q_P$ = permeate flow rate = buffer addition rate (so $V$ = constant). Rearranging:

$$\frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{Q_P}{V} dt = -\frac{dV_{\text{buffer}}}{V}$$

Integrating:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{c_f}{c_0}\right) = -\frac{V_{\text{buffer}}}{V} = -N$$

This confirms Step 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** Pharmaceutical industry routinely uses **\gt 8 diavolumes** when desalting mAb preparations, even though 4.6 volumes removes 99% of salt, because trace ionic contaminants can destabilize the protein during long-term storage. **Buffer cost is a real OPEX driver**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Crossflow Mass-Transfer Coefficient (Dériván–Gekas Form)

**Problem:** A UF module has a rectangular channel of width **2 cm**, height **0.4 cm**, and operates at crossflow velocity **0.5 m·s⁻¹**. The feed is a protein solution with $D = 6 \times 10^{-11}$ m²·s⁻¹, $\rho = 1000$ kg·m⁻³, $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the **mass-transfer coefficient** $k$.

**Step 1 — Compute the hydraulic diameter.**

$$d_h = \frac{4 \cdot a \cdot b}{2(a + b)} = \frac{2 \cdot a \cdot b}{a + b}$$

With $a = 0.01$ m, $b = 0.002$ m:

$$d_h = \frac{2 \times 0.01 \times 0.002}{0.01 + 0.002} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.012} = 3.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number.**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho u_c d_h}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.5 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{1.667}{0.001} = 1667$$

Since $\text{Re} < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**. So we use the laminar Sherwood correlation, not the turbulent one. For laminar flow in a rectangular duct:

$$\text{Sh} = 1.86 \left(\text{Re} \cdot \text{Sc} \cdot \frac{d_h}{L}\right)^{1/3}$$

Assume channel length $L = 1$ m (typical for module-scale).

**Step 3 — Compute Schmidt number.**

$$\text{Sc} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1000 \times 6 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.67 \times 10^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the argument of the cubic root.**

$$\text{Re} \cdot \text{Sc} \cdot \frac{d_h}{L} = 1667 \times 1.67 \times 10^{4} \times 3.33 \times 10^{-3}$$

Compute stepwise:

$$1667 \times 1.67 \times 10^{4} = 2.784 \times 10^{7}$$

$$2.784 \times 10^{7} \times 3.33 \times 10^{-3} = 9.27 \times 10^{4}$$

**Step 5 — Take the cube root.**

$$(9.27 \times 10^4)^{1/3} = (92{,}700)^{1/3} \approx 45.36$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Sherwood number.**

$$\text{Sh} = 1.86 \times 45.36 = 84.4$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $k$.**

$$k = \frac{\text{Sh} \cdot D}{d_h} = \frac{84.4 \times 6 \times 10^{-11}}{3.33 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In SI units**, $k \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m·s⁻¹, which corresponds to about **5.5 × 10⁻³ cm·s⁻¹** — typical for protein UF systems. Plug this into the gel-polarization equation to obtain the limiting flux for any given $c_g$ and bulk $c_b$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Operating Mode

| Goal | Best Mode | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Concentrate** a protein from 5 → 50 g/L | Batch concentration | High VCF achievable in single pass |
| **Desalt** a protein after ion-exchange elution | Constant-Volume Diafiltration (CVDF) | Volume held constant while small ions wash out |
| **Continuous production** at constant composition | Single-pass TFF or Feed-and-Bleed | Steady-state composition; large volume throughput |
| **Both concentrate AND buffer-exchange** | Sequential UF → CVDF → UF | Mimics the standard mAb downstream protocol |
| **Recover** a low-MW product from a fermentation broth | Nanofiltration or RO | Smaller solutes pass the membrane |
| **Recover** a high-MW product (protein) | UF with tight membrane | Protein retained; water and salts removed |
| **Concentrate heat-sensitive juice** without losing volatiles | RO or Osmotic Membrane Distillation | No phase change; ambient temperature |
| **Sterile filtration** of a cell-culture medium | Microfiltration (0.22 µm) | Removes bacteria and mycoplasma; protein passes |
