## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Reverse Osmosis**. How does it differ from **Ultrafiltration** in terms of (a) membrane structure, (b) operating pressure, and (c) species separated?

**Model Answer:**

**Reverse Osmosis (RO)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a hydraulic pressure greater than the **osmotic pressure difference** between feed and permeate is applied across a **dense (non-porous)** membrane to force the solvent (typically water) from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration. The membrane rejects dissolved ions and low-molecular-weight solutes primarily by a **solution-diffusion mechanism** rather than by size exclusion.

| Property | Reverse Osmosis | Ultrafiltration |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Membrane structure | Dense, non-porous (skin-type) | Porous, asymmetric with discrete pores |
| (b) Operating pressure | **15 – 80 bar** | **1 – 10 bar** |
| (c) Species separated | Monovalent ions, small molecules | Proteins, viruses, macromolecules (1–100 nm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking:** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain **Diafiltration**. State the equation for the **degree of separation** of a permeable solute as a function of the number of **diavolumes**.

**Model Answer:**

**Diafiltration** is a membrane operating mode in which a buffer (or pure solvent) is added to the retentate at the same rate as permeate is withdrawn, thereby **maintaining a constant retentate volume** while gradually removing small permeable solutes (e.g., salts, low-MW contaminants) from the solution. It is essentially a **washing operation** carried out on a **macrosolute** (e.g., protein) that is retained by the membrane.

The **degree of separation** of a fully permeable solute (sieving coefficient $\sigma_s = 1$) after $N$ diavolumes is:

$$\eta \;=\; 1 - \exp(-N)$$

where $N = V_{\text{buffer}} / V_{\text{retentate}}$ is the **number of diavolumes** (dimensionless).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking:** 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for the equation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**Q(a)** Derive the **van 't Hoff equation** for osmotic pressure and explain its **limitations** when applied to **biopolymer solutions** such as protein or polysaccharide feeds. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b)** A **Reverse Osmosis** unit processes a **NaCl feed at 2000 mg·L⁻¹** at **25 °C** with an applied pressure of **25 bar**. The membrane has pure-water permeability $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ and solute permeability $B = 4 \times 10^{-7}$ m·s⁻¹. The permeate concentration observed is **100 mg·L⁻¹**. Calculate: (i) osmotic pressure of the feed; (ii) net effective driving pressure; (iii) pure-water flux; (iv) observed rejection. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Q(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic basis of the van 't Hoff equation.**

Consider a semipermeable membrane separating pure solvent (side 1) from a solution (side 2) at temperature $T$. The condition of **thermodynamic equilibrium** requires the **chemical potential** of the solvent on both sides to be equal:

$$\mu_{\text{solvent}}^{(1)} \;=\; \mu_{\text{solvent}}^{(2)}$$

The chemical potential of the solvent in the solution is lowered by an **ideal-dilution** term $\ln(x_{\text{solvent}})$, where $x_{\text{solvent}}$ is the mole fraction of solvent. For an ideal solution:

$$\mu_{\text{solvent}}^{(2)} = \mu_{\text{solvent}}^{0} + RT \ln(x_{\text{solvent}})$$

The condition for equilibrium at zero hydraulic pressure is therefore:

$$\mu^{0} + RT \ln(x_{\text{solvent}}) = \mu^{0}$$

so the chemical potential drop must be balanced by applying a **hydrostatic pressure** $\pi$ that raises the chemical potential of the solution side back to equality. The full equilibrium condition is:

$$\mu^{0}(T, P^{0}) = \mu^{0}(T, P^{0} + \pi) + RT \ln(x_{\text{solvent}})$$

**Step 2 — Linearisation of the pressure dependence of $\mu$.**

For an incompressible solvent, the pressure dependence of the chemical potential is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = v_{\text{solvent}}$$

where $v_{\text{solvent}}$ is the **molar volume** of the solvent (m³·mol⁻¹). Integrating from $P^{0}$ to $P^{0} + \pi$:

$$\mu(T, P^{0}+\pi) - \mu(T, P^{0}) = v_{\text{solvent}} \cdot \pi$$

**Step 3 — Combine to derive $\pi$.**

The equilibrium condition becomes:

$$v_{\text{solvent}} \cdot \pi = -RT \ln(x_{\text{solvent}}) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{x_{\text{solvent}}}\right)$$

For a dilute solution, the **solvent mole fraction** can be approximated as:

$$x_{\text{solvent}} \approx 1 - \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \frac{c \cdot V}{c \cdot V + n_{\text{solvent}}}$$

For low concentrations, $\ln(1/x) \approx 1 - x \approx n_{\text{solute}} / n_{\text{solvent}}$. Multiplying by $n_{\text{solvent}} \approx V / v_{\text{solvent}}$ gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{x_{\text{solvent}}}\right) \approx \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{V / v_{\text{solvent}}} = v_{\text{solvent}} \cdot c$$

Substituting:

$$v_{\text{solvent}} \cdot \pi = RT \cdot v_{\text{solvent}} \cdot c$$

Cancelling $v_{\text{solvent}}$ on both sides:

$$\boxed{\;\pi \;=\; c \, R \, T\;}$$

This is the van 't Hoff equation. For dissociating electrolytes, multiplying by the **van 't Hoff factor $i$** accounts for the number of dissolved particles per formula unit:

$$\pi = i \cdot c \cdot R \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Limitations in biopolymer solutions.** **(3 marks)**

| Limitation | Reason |
|---|---|
| 1. Non-ideality | Biopolymers are **large** relative to solvent — they deviate strongly from Raoult's law; activity coefficients $\gamma \neq 1$. |
| 2. Concentration-dependent interactions | Above a few g·L⁻¹, protein–protein and protein–solvent interactions raise osmotic pressure **non-linearly**; virial expansion $\pi/cRT = 1 + B_2 c + B_3 c^2 + \ldots$ is required. |
| 3. Donnan effects | Charged biopolymers (e.g., proteins at pH $\neq$ pI) generate **counter-ion** distributions across the membrane, adding an osmotic term $\pi_{\text{Donnan}}$. |
| 4. Solvent structuring | Hydrophilic biopolymers organize water molecules, raising the **effective** concentration beyond the analytical concentration. |
| 5. Membrane–solute coupling | Biopolymers may adsorb on the membrane, locally concentrating and amplifying the effective osmotic pressure at the surface. |

Hence, for protein solutions encountered in UF, an **empirical virial-coefficient form** $\pi = RT(c/A_2 + c^2/A_3 + \ldots)$ is preferred.

---

### Model Answer — Q(b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $c_F = 2000 \text{ mg·L}^{-1} = 2 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$
- $T = 25\,°\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- $\Delta P_{\text{applied}} = 25 \text{ bar} = 25 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$
- $B = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$
- $c_P = 100 \text{ mg·L}^{-1} = 0.1 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$
- $i = 2$ for NaCl
- $M_{\text{NaCl}} = 58.44 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$

**Step (i) — Osmotic pressure of the feed.**

Convert $c_F$ to mol·m⁻³:

$$c_F = \frac{2 \text{ g·L}^{-1}}{58.44 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}} \times \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} = \frac{2000}{58.44} = 34.22 \text{ mol·m}^{-3}$$

Apply the van 't Hoff equation:

$$\pi_F = i \cdot c_F \cdot R \cdot T = 2 \times 34.22 \times 8.314 \times 298.15$$

Compute stepwise:

$$2 \times 34.22 = 68.44$$

$$68.44 \times 8.314 = 568.95$$

$$568.95 \times 298.15 \approx 1.696 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 1.696 \text{ bar}$$

So $\pi_F \approx 1.70$ bar.

**Step (ii) — Net effective driving pressure.**

Assume the permeate is essentially pure water so $\pi_P \approx 0$. Then:

$$\Delta \pi = \pi_F - \pi_P \approx 1.70 \text{ bar}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P_{\text{applied}} - \Delta \pi = 25 - 1.70 = 23.30 \text{ bar} = 23.30 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step (iii) — Pure-water flux through the membrane.**

$$J_w = A \cdot \Delta P_{\text{eff}}$$

$$J_w = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \times 23.30 \times 10^{5} = 1.2 \times 23.30 \times 10^{-2} = 0.2796 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$$

Wait — careful with units. $A$ has units m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹, so:

$$J_w = (1.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}) \times (23.30 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}) = 1.2 \times 23.30 \times 10^{-2} = 0.2796 \text{ m·s}^{-1}$$

Convert to LMH:

$$J_w = 0.2796 \text{ m·s}^{-1} \times \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} \times \frac{3600 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ h}} = 0.2796 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1} = 1.006 \times 10^{6} \text{ LMH}$$

That is unrealistically high — so either the actual $\Delta\pi$ is much closer to the applied pressure (concentrate side), or we should report in SI: **$J_w \approx 0.28$ m·s⁻¹** which corresponds to roughly **$10^6$ LMH**. The point is that the **SI flux is huge** because RO membranes are extremely thin (~100 nm active layer) and $A$ values are high.

For a more realistic answer, RO membranes have $J \approx 10 - 30$ LMH at 25 bar, so the actual $A$ for such a membrane would be about $10^{-11}$ m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹. The calculation above is **mathematically correct**; the unrealistically large result simply reflects the choice of $A$.

**Step (iv) — Observed rejection.**

$$R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \frac{c_P}{c_F} = 1 - \frac{100}{2000} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

So the rejection is **95%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** When applying the van 't Hoff equation, students often use $c_F$ in mol·L⁻¹ **without converting to mol·m⁻³**. This introduces a factor-of-1000 error. Always convert to SI before inserting $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. **Alternative trap:** forgetting to multiply by $i$ for NaCl — gives an osmotic pressure **half** the correct value.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**Q(a)** Define **Ultrafiltration**. Derive the **gel-polarization model** for limiting flux. List **three back-transport mechanisms** that prevent further flux decline at high concentrations. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b)** A **single-pass crossflow UF** system processes **1000 L·h⁻¹** of a protein feed at **5 g·L⁻¹**, reducing it to a retentate at **40 g·L⁻¹** with a permeate containing **0.05 g·L⁻¹** protein. The crossflow channel is rectangular with width 4 cm, height 0.5 cm. The bulk crossflow velocity is **0.6 m·s⁻¹**, and the gel concentration for the protein is $c_g = 200$ g·L⁻¹. Protein diffusivity is $6 \times 10^{-11}$ m²·s⁻¹; $\rho = 1000$ kg·m⁻³, $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Estimate: (i) the average permeate flux (assuming the gel model holds); (ii) the membrane area required; (iii) the channel flow regime (Re). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Q(a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**

**Ultrafiltration (UF)** is a pressure-driven membrane process using porous membranes with pore sizes in the range **1 – 100 nm** at operating pressures of **1 – 10 bar**, used to concentrate and fractionate macromolecules such as proteins, viruses, and polysaccharides while passing water, salts, and small organic molecules.

**Derivation of Gel-Polarization Model (3 marks):**

Consider a macrosolute (e.g., protein) being convected toward a UF membrane by the permeate flow of velocity $J$. At steady state, the rate of convective solute transport toward the membrane equals the rate of back-diffusion away from the membrane plus the rate of solute leaving with the permeate:

$$J \cdot c(x) \;=\; -D \cdot \frac{dc}{dx} \;+\; J \cdot c_P$$

In most UF, $c_P \approx 0$ (macrosolute is fully rejected). So:

$$J \cdot c(x) \;=\; -D \cdot \frac{dc}{dx}$$

This is a first-order ODE. Rearranging:

$$J \, dx \;=\; -D \, \frac{dc}{c}$$

Integrating from the bulk ($x = 0$, $c = c_b$) to the membrane surface ($x = \delta$, $c = c_w = c_g$):

$$J \int_{0}^{\delta} dx \;=\; -D \int_{c_b}^{c_g} \frac{dc}{c}$$

$$J \cdot \delta \;=\; D \ln\!\left(\frac{c_b}{c_g}\right)$$

Recognising that $D / \delta = k$, the **mass-transfer coefficient**:

$$\boxed{\;J_{\lim} \;=\; k \ln\!\left(\frac{c_g}{c_b}\right)\;}$$

This is the **limiting flux equation** of UF — flux is independent of pressure once $c_w$ reaches the gel concentration $c_g$.

**Three Back-Transport Mechanisms (2 marks):**

| Mechanism | Applicable Particle Size | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Brownian diffusion** | Small colloids (\lt 0.1 µm) | $J \propto \dot{\gamma}_w^{0}$ (gel model) |
| **Shear-induced diffusion** | Intermediate (0.5 – 50 µm) | $J \propto \dot{\gamma}_w^{0.33}$ |
| **Inertial (lateral) lift** | Large particles (\gt 50 µm) | $J \propto \dot{\gamma}_w^{1.5}$ |

---

### Model Answer — Q(b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $Q_F = 1000 \text{ L·h}^{-1}$, $c_F = 5 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$, $c_R = 40 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$, $c_P = 0.05 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$
- $w = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.5 \text{ cm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$
- $u_c = 0.6 \text{ m·s}^{-1}$
- $c_g = 200 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$, $D = 6 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2\text{·s}^{-1}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg·m}^{-3}$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$

**Step (i) — Average permeate flux.**

Average the bulk concentration over the length of the module (geometric mean is a standard engineering approximation):

$$\overline{c_b} = \sqrt{c_F \cdot c_R} = \sqrt{5 \times 40} = \sqrt{200} = 14.14 \text{ g·L}^{-1}$$

Convert to kg·m⁻³: $14.14 \text{ g·L}^{-1} = 14.14 \text{ kg·m}^{-3}$.

Hydraulic diameter:

$$d_h = \frac{2 \cdot a \cdot b}{a+b} = \frac{2 \times 0.02 \times 0.0025}{0.02 + 0.0025} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0225} = 4.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

Reynolds number:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{1000 \times 0.6 \times 4.44 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = 2667$$

Since $\text{Re} > 2300$, the flow is **turbulent**. Use the Dériván–Gekas turbulent Sherwood correlation:

$$\text{Sh} = 0.023 \cdot \text{Re}^{0.875} \cdot \text{Sc}^{0.25}$$

Schmidt number:

$$\text{Sc} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1000 \times 6 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.667 \times 10^{4}$$

Compute:

$$\text{Re}^{0.875} = 2667^{0.875}$$

Use $\log_{10}(2667) = 3.426$. Then $0.875 \times 3.426 = 2.998$, so $\text{Re}^{0.875} \approx 10^{2.998} \approx 994$.

$$\text{Sc}^{0.25} = (1.667 \times 10^4)^{0.25}$$

$\log_{10}(16670) = 4.222$; $0.25 \times 4.222 = 1.055$; $\text{Sc}^{0.25} \approx 11.35$.

$$\text{Sh} = 0.023 \times 994 \times 11.35 \approx 0.023 \times 11281 \approx 259.5$$

Mass-transfer coefficient:

$$k = \frac{\text{Sh} \cdot D}{d_h} = \frac{259.5 \times 6 \times 10^{-11}}{4.44 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.557 \times 10^{-8}}{4.44 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.51 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$$

Now apply the gel model:

$$J = k \ln\!\left(\frac{c_g}{\overline{c_b}}\right) = 3.51 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{200}{14.14}\right) = 3.51 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln(14.14)$$

$\ln(14.14) = 2.649$:

$$J \approx 3.51 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.649 = 9.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$$

Convert to LMH:

$$J = 9.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m·s}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 33.5 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$$

So $J \approx 33.5$ LMH.

**Step (ii) — Membrane area.**

From the mass balance, the permeate flow rate equals the retentate loss:

$$Q_P = Q_F - Q_R$$

Solute balance: $Q_F c_F = Q_R c_R + Q_P c_P$. Assuming $c_P$ is negligible:

$$Q_R \approx Q_F \cdot \frac{c_F}{c_R} = 1000 \times \frac{5}{40} = 125 \text{ L·h}^{-1}$$

So $Q_P = 1000 - 125 = 875 \text{ L·h}^{-1}$.

Membrane area:

$$A_m = \frac{Q_P}{J} = \frac{875 \text{ L·h}^{-1}}{33.5 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}} = 26.1 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (iii) — Flow regime.**

Already computed in (i): $\text{Re} = 2667$, which is **just turbulent** ($\text{Re} > 2300$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $i$ factor** in van 't Hoff for salts → osmotic pressure is **half** of the correct value.
> 2. **Using $c_F$ instead of $c_R$** when computing osmotic pressure for an RO module operating at steady state → underestimates $\Delta\pi$ by a large factor when the system is well-rejected.
> 3. **Mixing LMH and SI units** in the membrane area equation → off by a factor of $\mathbf{3.6 \times 10^6}$.
> 4. **Forgetting that $c_g$ depends on the membrane-solute pair** — it is NOT a universal constant. Values range from ~100 g/L for compact globular proteins to ~600 g/L for highly soluble polysaccharides.
> 5. **In laminar crossflow, using the turbulent Sherwood correlation** — gives a $k$ value too high by 30–100%.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST-KNOW KEY POINTS BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

- [x] **Membrane separation** is a *rate-controlled* (kinetic) process, not equilibrium-based.
- [x] All pressure-driven membrane processes share the **flux form** $J = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ but only RO has significant $\Delta\pi$ because only RO rejects small solutes.
- [x] **Van 't Hoff equation** $\pi = i c R T$ applies ONLY to ideal dilute solutions — for proteins and polysaccharides, a **virial expansion** is required.
- [x] **Osmotic pressure of seawater** ($\approx$ 0.6 M NaCl) is about **25 bar** — this is why seawater RO uses **\gt 60 bar**.
- [x] **Rejection** $R = 1 - c_P / c_F$ is the OBSERVED value. The TRUE rejection uses $c_M$ (membrane-surface concentration), which is HIGHER than $c_F$ due to concentration polarization.
- [x] **Dialysis** is diffusion-driven, slow, and only exchanges solutes — does NOT concentrate the retentate.
- [x] **Ultrafiltration gel-polarization model:** $J = k \ln(c_g / c_b)$ — independent of pressure at steady state; the gel concentration $c_g$ is a property of the *membrane–solute pair*.
- [x] **Resistance-in-series model** for fouled UF: $J = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)]$. Pore plugging ($R_f$) is irreversible; cake ($R_c$) is partially removable by crossflow.
- [x] **Crossflow** reduces cake formation by generating wall shear $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8 u_c / d_h$; higher $\dot{\gamma}_w$ → higher back-transport → higher limiting flux.
- [x] **Three back-transport mechanisms:** Brownian diffusion (small), shear-induced diffusion (medium), inertial lift (large particles).
- [x] **Re vs. flow regime:** $\text{Re} \lt 2300$ laminar; $\text{Re} > 2300$ turbulent (in ducts).
- [x] **Sherwood correlations:**
  - Laminar duct: $\text{Sh} = 1.86 (\text{Re} \cdot \text{Sc} \cdot d_h/L)^{1/3}$ (Graetz–Leveque)
  - Turbulent duct: $\text{Sh} = 0.023 \text{Re}^{0.875} \text{Sc}^{0.25}$ (Dériván–Gekas)
- [x] **Diafiltration separation:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-N)$, where $N = V_{\text{buffer}}/V_{\text{retentate}}$ (diavolumes).
- [x] **Protein recovery during concentration:** $Y = (V_f / V_0)^{\sigma - 1}$. For a fully rejected protein ($\sigma = 0$), $Y = 1$ — no loss.
- [x] **Standard mAb downstream protocol:** UF (concentrate) → CVDF (8–10 diavolumes buffer exchange) → UF (final formulation concentration).
- [x] **Hagen-Poiseuille reminder:** flux $\propto r_p^4$ in UF — small pore-size changes have **dramatic** flux consequences.
- [x] **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** — universal gas constant; remember SI units for $c$ (mol·m⁻³) and $T$ (K).
- [x] **Membrane area equation:** $A_m = V_p / (J \cdot t)$ — design trade-off: bigger area → shorter time → higher CAPEX.
- [x] **Applications — quick recall:**
  - MF: sterile filtration, cell harvesting
  - UF: protein concentration, virus removal, mAb formulation
  - NF: water softening, dye removal, antibiotic purification
  - RO: desalination, water for injection (WFI), boiler feed
  - Dialysis: hemodialysis, lab desalting

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion-Exchange Processes-Physical Description, Separation Mechanisms, Equipment and Applications, Aqueous-Two Phase Separations-Theory, Methods and Applications, Precipitation-Theory, Design/Methods, Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# I. Ion-Exchange Processes

## 1.1 Formal Definition & Physical Description

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT865 - ADVANCED BIO SEPARATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ion-Exchange Processes-Physical Description, Separation Mechanisms, Equipment and Applications, Aqueous-Two Phase Separations-Theory, Methods and Applications, Precipitation-Theory, Design/Methods, Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (High-Yield)**
> **Ion-exchange** is a **stoichiometric, reversible** sorption process in which ions of like charge (cations or anions) are exchanged between a **solid, insoluble, high-molecular-weight polymeric matrix** (the ion-exchange *resin*) and a **liquid electrolyte solution**, driven by **electrostatic (Coulombic) interactions** without any permanent change in the physical structure of the resin.

### Physical Description of the System

The ion-exchange system consists of three fundamental physical components:

1. **The Solid Phase (Resin/Matrix):**
   - A **cross-linked polymer matrix** (usually polystyrene-divinylbenzene — *DVB* — or cellulose-based).
   - Contains fixed ionic functional groups covalently bonded to the backbone.
   - Pore structure: **Gel-type (microporous)** or **Macroporous (macroreticular)**.
   - Typical particle size: **50–500 μm**; bead or granular form.

2. **The Mobile Counter-Ion (Exchangeable Ion):**
   - Loosely bound to the resin; can be displaced by ions of higher affinity.
   - Located in the **electrical double layer** of the resin.

3. **The External Liquid Phase (Electrolyte Solution):**
   - Contains the **target ionic solute** to be captured.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Reservation" Analogy

Imagine a **swanky restaurant** (the resin bead) with a fixed number of tables (fixed ionic groups). Each table currently holds a guest (counter-ion) — let's call them sodium (Na⁺). 

When a more "VIP" customer (like a **protein**, **Ca²⁺**, or **antibiotic molecule**) arrives in the surrounding lobby (the liquid), the Na⁺ guests are politely asked to leave and go to the lobby. The VIP takes the table. This swap is **stoichiometric** (one VIP = one Na⁺ displaced to maintain charge neutrality).

When the restaurant is full, the bead must be **regenerated** by flooding with concentrated NaCl — the high concentration of Na⁺ "kicks out" the VIPs, restoring the resin for another cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Ion-exchange = *"Reservation Swap"* — fixed seats, mobile guests, electroneutrality always preserved.

### Categories of Ion-Exchangers

| Type | Functional Group | Exchangeable Ion | Typical Use |
|------|------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Strong Acid Cation (SAC)** | –SO₃⁻ H⁺ | H⁺ (or Na⁺) | Water softening, amino acid capture |
| **Weak Acid Cation (WAC)** | –COO⁻ H⁺ | H⁺ | Antibiotic purification (gentamicin) |
| **Strong Base Anion (SBA)** | –N⁺(CH₃)₃ OH⁻ | OH⁻ (or Cl⁻) | Removal of nucleic acids, endotoxins |
| **Weak Base Anion (WBA)** | –NH₂ / –NHR | H⁺ in acid medium | Decolorization, organic acid removal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Capacity:** Resin capacity is reported as **eq/L of wet resin** or **meq/g dry resin**. A typical strong cation resin has a capacity of **≈ 1.8–2.0 eq/L**.

---

# II. Aqueous Two-Phase Separations (ATPS)

## 2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (High-Yield)**
> **Aqueous Two-Phase Separation (ATPS)** is a **liquid–liquid extraction technique** in which an aqueous solution of a biological feed separates, above a critical concentration, into **two immiscible aqueous-rich phases**, each predominantly composed of water (>70% w/w), with partitioning of biomolecules (proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids, organelles) governed by differences in their **relative affinity** for the two phases.

## 2.2 Physical Description

Unlike organic-aqueous systems, ATPS uses **two water-soluble polymers** (or one polymer + one salt) so both phases are gentle and **biocompatible** — biomolecules retain their native activity.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Oil & Water" Myth Busted

Think of a salad dressing: normally oil and water separate. Now imagine two **thirsty sponges** (polymers) thrown into a beaker of water. Each sponge grabs water molecules differently. One sponge (PEG) is **hydrophilic and loosely binds water**; the other (dextran) is **denser and binds water tightly**. Eventually the sponges repel each other and form **two water-soaked layers** — both still 70–90% water! Proteins "choose" which sponge-bath they prefer.

---

# III. Precipitation

## 3.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (High-Yield)**
> **Precipitation** in bioseparations is a **bulk, low-resolution separation technique** in which a dissolved solute (typically a protein or biomolecule) is rendered **insoluble** in an aqueous mother liquor by changing one or more intensive variables — **pH, temperature, ionic strength, dielectric constant, or antisolvent concentration** — followed by **solid–liquid separation** (filtration/centrifugation) to recover the solid phase.

## 3.2 Physical Description

Three ingredients are always required: **(i) the solute**, **(ii) the solvent (water)**, and **(iii) the precipitant** (salt, organic solvent, or pH-shifting acid/base).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Block in Cold Soup"

Picture a Lego (protein) floating happily in warm chicken soup. As you add **a fistful of salt (ammonium sulfate)** while stirring, the salt "steals" the water molecules that were hydrating the Lego. The Lego dehydrates, and several Legos clump together ("salting-out"). They become too heavy to stay suspended — they **rain down (precipitate)** to the bottom of the pot.

---

# IV. Summary Callout for Module 2 — Three Major Recovery Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Goal:** All three techniques — ion-exchange, ATPS, and precipitation — belong to the **product-isolation / concentration** stage of a downstream processing train, *preceding* a final high-resolution step (e.g., HPLC or crystallization).

| Technique | Driving Force | Resolution | Scale | Cost |
|-----------|---------------|------------|-------|------|
| Ion-Exchange | Electrostatic (Coulombic) | **High** | Industrial | Moderate |
| ATPS | Hydrophobic / Electrostatic / Biospecific | Medium | Lab–Pilot | Low–Moderate |
| Precipitation | Solubility change | **Low** | Industrial | **Very Low** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# V. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 5.1 Ion-Exchange — Theoretical Foundations

### 5.1.1 The Exchange Reaction (Generalized)

For a cation exchanger initially in the **A-form** contacting a solution containing **B⁺** ions:

$$\text{R–A} + \text{B}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \text{R–B} + \text{A}^{+}$$

where **R⁻** denotes the fixed anionic site on the resin matrix. The **electroneutrality** constraint is **absolute** — one A⁺ released for every B⁺ bound.

For multivalent ions, stoichiometry must balance valence:

$$\text{R}_2\text{–A} + \text{B}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{–B} + 2\,\text{A}^{+}$$

### 5.1.2 Selectivity Coefficient (KTU Hot Topic)

The **selectivity coefficient** ($K_A^B$) quantifies the resin's preference for B⁺ over A⁺:

$$K_A^B = \frac{[\text{B}^+]_R \cdot [\text{A}^+]_S}{[\text{A}^+]_R \cdot [\text{B}^+]_S}$$

- If $K_A^B > 1$ → resin **prefers B⁺**.
- If $K_A^B < 1$ → resin **prefers A⁺**.

### 5.1.3 Mass Action Law & Equilibrium Loading

For a monovalent exchange, the equilibrium expression can be written in terms of **equivalent ionic fractions**:

$$\frac{q_B}{q_A} = K_A^B \cdot \frac{C_B}{C_A}$$

where $q_i$ are the loadings (eq/g resin) and $C_i$ are solution concentrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Selectivity generally follows the **Hofmeister / lyotropic series**:
> **Al³⁺ > Ca²⁺ > Mg²⁺ > K⁺ > Na⁺ > H⁺ > Li⁺** (for strong acid cation exchangers).

### 5.1.4 Breakthrough Curve & Operating Capacity

In a packed column of height $H$, the breakthrough time $t_b$ is given by:

$$t_b = \frac{V \cdot (q_{eq} - q_{0})}{C_0 \cdot Q} - \frac{\epsilon \cdot V}{Q}$$

where:
- $V$ = bed volume (L)
- $q_{eq}$ = equilibrium loading (eq/L bed)
- $C_0$ = inlet solute concentration
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate
- $\epsilon$ = bed porosity (typically **0.35–0.45**)

### 5.1.5 The Plate Theory (Number of Theoretical Plates)

Resolution increases with the **number of transfer units (NTU)**:

$$N = \frac{L}{HETP}$$

$$HETP = H + \frac{C_1 u}{C_2 + u}$$

where $H$ = fixed contribution, $C_1, C_2$ = kinetic constants, $u$ = superficial velocity. **Lower HETP → sharper breakthrough → better utilization.**

---

## 5.2 Aqueous Two-Phase Separation — Theoretical Foundations

### 5.2.1 Binodal Curve & Critical Point

The phase diagram plots polymer concentration (e.g., % PEG, w/w) on both axes. The **binodal curve** divides the single-phase (below) from the two-phase region (above). A **tie-line** connects the equilibrium compositions of the top and bottom phases.

Length of tie-line $\Delta$ correlates with **partition strength**:

$$\Delta = \sqrt{(C_{PEG,T} - C_{PEG,B})^2 + (C_{DEX,T} - C_{DEX,B})^2}$$

### 5.2.2 The Partition Coefficient (K — KTU Hot Topic)

$$K = \frac{C_T}{C_B}$$

- $C_T$ = concentration of target biomolecule in the **top phase**
- $C_B$ = concentration in the **bottom phase**
- $K > 1$ → biomolecule partitions preferentially to top phase
- $K < 1$ → partitions to bottom phase

### 5.2.3 Volume Ratio & Recovery

Volume ratio $R$:

$$R = \frac{V_T}{V_B}$$

**Recovery** (in top phase):

$$Y_T = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1}{R \cdot K}\right)}$$

**Purification factor:**

$$PF = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K-1}{R+1}\right) \cdot \text{(contaminant K)}}$$

### 5.2.4 Theoretical Models (KTU Frequently Asked)

1. **Bronsted Partition Theory** — based on interfacial tension and molecular hydrophobicity.
2. **Flory-Huggins Lattice Theory** — polymer-polymer interaction parameter $\chi_{AB}$ governs phase separation:

$$\Delta G_{mix} = RT \left( n_A \ln \phi_A + n_B \ln \phi_B + n_A \phi_B \chi_{AB} \right)$$

A positive $\chi_{AB}$ drives phase separation.

3. **Albertsson's Empirical Correlations** — using **hydrophobicity**, **charge**, **molecular weight** to predict K.

---

## 5.3 Precipitation — Theoretical Foundations

### 5.3.1 The Solubility Equation (Cohn Equation — KTU Must-Know)

The classical Cohn equation expresses the **logarithm of protein solubility** as a function of ionic strength:

$$\log S = \log S_0 - K_S \cdot I$$

where:
- $S$ = solubility at ionic strength $I$
- $S_0$ = solubility at $I = 0$ (extrapolated)
- $K_S$ = **salting-out constant** (specific to each protein)
- $I$ = ionic strength

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High $K_S$ values = "easy-to-salt-out" proteins**. Typical values lie between **0.5 and 2.5**.

### 5.3.2 Ionic Strength Definition

$$I = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i z_i^2$$

### 5.3.3 Common Precipitation Strategies (KTU Board Favourite)

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Reagent |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Salting-out** | Hydration shell competition | (NH₄)₂SO₄, Na₂SO₄ |
| **Isoelectric precipitation** | pH → pI → zero net charge | HCl, NaOH, acetic acid |
| **Organic solvent** | Dielectric constant reduction | Ethanol, acetone, isopropanol |
| **Polyelectrolyte** | Bridging/flocculation | Polyacrylic acid, polyethyleneimine |
| **Metal-ion** | Bridge formation with COOH | Ca²⁺, Mn²⁺, Zn²⁺ |
| **Affinity precip.** | Ligand-mediated bis-molecular capture | Antibody-hapten conjugates |

### 5.3.4 Supersaturation & Nucleation Theory

The fundamental driving force for precipitation is **supersaturation, S**:

$$S = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*}$$

- $S < 0$ → undersaturated
- $0 < S < 1$ → metastable (growth dominant)
- $S > 1$ → labile (nucleation dominant)

**Primary nucleation rate J:**

$$J = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B \cdot \gamma^3}{(\ln S)^2 (k_BT)^3}\right)$$

where $\gamma$ = interfacial tension, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant. **High $S$ → fine, amorphous, impure precipitate; Low $S$ → large, crystalline, pure precipitate.**

### 5.3.5 Selectivity & Yield — The Trade-off

For binary protein mixtures, **selectivity** $S_{1,2}$:

$$S_{1,2} = \frac{S_2^{0.5}}{S_1^{0.5}}$$

**Yield** $Y$:

$$Y = 1 - \frac{S}{S_0}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Master Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Table Readability:** All vertical bars use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Selectivity Coefficient | $K_A^B = \dfrac{q_B \cdot C_A}{q_A \cdot C_B}$ | dimensionless | $K_A^B > 1$ → resin prefers B |
| 2 | Breakthrough Time | $t_b = \dfrac{V(q_{eq}-q_0)}{C_0 Q} - \dfrac{\epsilon V}{Q}$ | seconds | typical $\epsilon = 0.4$ |
| 3 | Number of Plates | $N = \dfrac{L}{HETP}$ | dimensionless | higher $N$ → sharper band |
| 4 | ATPS Partition Coeff. | $K = \dfrac{C_T}{C_B}$ | dimensionless | $K \gg 1$ or $K \ll 1$ ideal |
| 5 | Volume Ratio | $R = \dfrac{V_T}{V_B}$ | dimensionless | usually 0.5–2 |
| 6 | Recovery (top phase) | $Y_T = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{RK}}$ | fraction | 0 to 1 |
| 7 | Flory-Huggins Free Energy | $\Delta G_{mix} = RT \sum n_i \ln \phi_i + RT\chi_{AB} n_A \phi_B$ | J/mol | $\chi_{AB} > 0$ → immiscible |
| 8 | Cohn Solubility | $\log S = \log S_0 - K_S \cdot I$ | log scale | linear in $I$ |
| 9 | Ionic Strength | $I = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ | mol/L | monovalent salt: $I = c$ |
| 10 | Supersaturation | $S = \dfrac{C - C^*}{C^*}$ | dimensionless | $S > 1$ precipitates |
| 11 | Nucleation Rate | $J = A \exp\left[ - \dfrac{B \gamma^3}{(\ln S)^2 (k_BT)^3} \right]$ | nuclei/m³·s | exponential sensitivity |
| 12 | Selectivity (precipitation) | $S_{1,2} = \dfrac{S_2^{0.5}}{S_1^{0.5}}$ | dimensionless | $> 1$ selective |
| 13 | Yield | $Y = 1 - \dfrac{S}{S_0}$ | fraction | approaches 1 at high precipitant |

---

## 5.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ion-exchange** is the **workhorse of industrial water softening** (capacity >10⁶ L treated per m³ resin) and **pharmaceutical antibiotic capture** (gentamicin, streptomycin via WAC resins).
- **ATPS** is exploited commercially in **monoclonal antibody purification** and **plasmid DNA recovery** — its biocompatibility makes it greener than organic solvent extraction.
- **Precipitation** dominates **blood plasma fractionation (Cohn Process, 1946)** — still the **basis of modern albumin and IgG manufacture**, with ethanol fractions V/II + III processed at multi-ton scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# VI. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 6.1 Worked Example 1 — Ion-Exchange Capacity & Selectivity (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A strong cation exchange resin in the H⁺ form (10.0 g dry weight, exchange capacity = 4.5 meq/g) is contacted with 500 mL of a solution containing **120 meq of Na⁺** at pH 7. The selectivity coefficient $K_{H}^{Na}$ = **1.5**. Calculate the equilibrium loading of Na⁺ on the resin and the residual Na⁺ in solution.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Total Resin Capacity**

$$Q_{tot} = m_{resin} \times \text{capacity} = 10.0 \text{ g} \times 4.5 \text{ meq/g} = 45.0 \text{ meq}$$

**Step 2 — Mass-Balance (electroneutrality)**

$$\boxed{n_{Na,R} + n_{H,R} = 45.0 \text{ meq}}$$

$$n_{Na,S} + n_{H,S} = 120 \text{ meq (initial Na)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Selectivity Equation**

$$K_{H}^{Na} = \frac{n_{Na,R} \cdot n_{H,S}}{n_{H,R} \cdot n_{Na,S}} = 1.5$$

**Step 4 — Substitute H⁺ from Resin** → $n_{H,R} = 45 - n_{Na,R}$

$$\frac{n_{Na,R} \cdot n_{H,S}}{(45 - n_{Na,R}) \cdot n_{Na,S}} = 1.5$$

**Step 5 — Apply Conservation**

$n_{Na,S} = 120 - n_{Na,R}$ and $n_{H,S} = n_{Na,R}$ (each Na bound releases 1 H⁺).

Substitute:

$$\frac{n_{Na,R} \cdot n_{Na,R}}{(45 - n_{Na,R})(120 - n_{Na,R})} = 1.5$$

**Step 6 — Let x = $n_{Na,R}$, Rearrange to Quadratic**

$$x^2 = 1.5 (45 - x)(120 - x)$$

$$x^2 = 1.5 (5400 - 45x - 120x + x^2)$$

$$x^2 = 1.5 (5400 - 165x + x^2)$$

$$x^2 = 8100 - 247.5 x + 1.5 x^2$$

Bring to one side:

$$0 = 8100 - 247.5 x + 0.5 x^2$$

Multiply by 2:

$$x^2 - 495 x + 16200 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve Quadratic**

$$x = \frac{495 \pm \sqrt{495^2 - 4 \cdot 16200}}{2}$$

$$x = \frac{495 \pm \sqrt{245025 - 64800}}{2}$$

$$x = \frac{495 \pm \sqrt{180225}}{2} = \frac{495 \pm 424.53}{2}$$

**Two roots:** $x = 459.77$ (rejected, exceeds resin capacity) and $x = 35.23$ meq.

**Step 8 — Equilibrium Loading**

$$\boxed{n_{Na,R} \approx 35.2 \text{ meq} \Rightarrow q_{Na} = 3.52 \text{ meq/g}}$$

**Step 9 — Residual Na⁺ in Solution**

$$n_{Na,S} = 120 - 35.2 = 84.8 \text{ meq}$$

$$C_{Na,S} = \frac{84.8 \text{ meq}}{0.500 \text{ L}} = \boxed{169.6 \text{ meq/L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Only 78% of resin capacity was utilized — indicating a **sub-saturating feed** ($C_{Na}/Q$ ratio too low). In industry, a **higher feed-to-resin ratio** would saturate the bed.

---

## 6.2 Worked Example 2 — ATPS Partition Coefficient & Yield

> **Problem:** A **PEG 4000 / dextran T500** ATPS system is prepared with **8% PEG** and **8% dextran** (w/w). After mixing with a cell lysate, the top (PEG-rich) phase volume is **40 mL** and the bottom (dextran-rich) phase volume is **60 mL**. The enzyme activity measured is **120 U/mL in top phase** and **15 U/mL in bottom phase**. Compute: (a) Partition coefficient K, (b) Volume ratio R, (c) Recovery in top phase, and (d) Total enzyme units recovered.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step (a) Partition Coefficient**

$$K = \frac{C_T}{C_B} = \frac{120 \text{ U/mL}}{15 \text{ U/mL}} = \boxed{8.0}$$

**Interpretation:** Enzyme strongly prefers the PEG-rich (top) phase.

**Step (b) Volume Ratio**

$$R = \frac{V_T}{V_B} = \frac{40}{60} = \boxed{0.667}$$

**Step (c) Recovery (Top Phase)**

$$Y_T = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{R \cdot K}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{0.667 \times 8.0}}$$

$$Y_T = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{5.333}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1875} = \frac{1}{1.1875}$$

$$Y_T = \boxed{0.842 \text{ or } 84.2\%}$$

**Step (d) Total Enzyme Units Recovered in Top Phase**

$$\text{Total U} = C_T \times V_T = 120 \text{ U/mL} \times 40 \text{ mL} = \boxed{4800 \text{ U}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** With K = 8 and R = 0.667, recovery is high (84%). However, the bottom phase still contains $15 \times 60 = 900$ U (15.8% loss). To recover this, an **inverse-phase ATPS** (with reverse salt concentration) or a **second-stage extraction** may be added.

---

## 6.3 Worked Example 3 — Cohn Precipitation & Salting-Out Calculation

> **Problem:** Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is precipitated from a 2.0 L solution (initial concentration = **8.0 mg/mL**) using ammonium sulfate at 4 °C. The Cohn equation for BSA at pH 7 gives $S_0$ = **260 mg/mL** and $K_S$ = **1.10**. Determine the **ionic strength** required to precipitate **90% of the BSA**, and the **amount of (NH₄)₂SO₄** (MW = 132.14 g/mol) needed.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Determine Target Solubility S**

For 90% precipitation, residual solubility = 10% of the original load would be in solution at equilibrium, but here we work with the **Cohn equation directly**.

We want only 10% of the original to remain soluble → final dissolved BSA = 0.10 × 8.0 = **0.8 mg/mL**.

However, the Cohn equation gives the *intrinsic solubility* $S$ at a given ionic strength. At low ionic strength (no salt added), $S \approx S_0 = 260$ mg/mL — but our initial concentration is only 8 mg/mL. The salt reduces $S$ until $S < 8$, causing precipitation.

For 90% yield: residual in solution = 0.8 mg/mL → set **S = 0.8 mg/mL** (treated as equilibrium solubility limit).

**Step 2 — Apply Cohn Equation**

$$\log S = \log S_0 - K_S \cdot I$$

$$\log(0.8) = \log(260) - 1.10 \cdot I$$

$$-0.0969 = 2.4150 - 1.10 \cdot I$$

$$1.10 \cdot I = 2.4150 + 0.0969 = 2.5119$$

$$\boxed{I = 2.284 \text{ M}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert I to (NH₄)₂SO₄ Mass**

For a strong electrolyte dissociating into 2 monovalent + 1 divalent: $(NH_4)_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2 NH_4^+ + SO_4^{2-}$

$I = \frac{1}{2} \sum c_i z_i^2 = \frac{1}{2}[c(1)^2 + c(1)^2 + c(2)^2] = \frac{1}{2}[2c + 4c] = 3c$

Therefore $c_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = \dfrac{I}{3} = \dfrac{2.284}{3} = 0.761$ M

**Step 4 — Mass Calculation**

$$\text{moles} = 0.761 \text{ mol/L} \times 2.0 \text{ L} = 1.523 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{mass} = 1.523 \times 132.14 = \boxed{201.2 \text{ g of } (NH_4)_2SO_4}$$

This is the mass to be added to **2.0 L** of BSA solution.

**Step 5 — Sanity Check (Yield)**

Recovered BSA mass = 0.90 × (8 mg/mL × 2000 mL) = 0.90 × 16,000 mg = **14,400 mg = 14.4 g**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing the *ionic strength of the added salt solution* with the *ionic strength of the mixture*. The above calculation assumes that the contribution from BSA buffer ions is negligible compared to the added ammonium sulfate — a valid assumption only when the precipitation dominates the ionic environment.

---

## 6.4 Worked Example 4 — Breakthrough Curve Scaling

> **Problem:** A glass column (diameter 2.5 cm, packed resin height 30 cm) is loaded with a strong cation exchanger ($q_{eq}$ = 1.8 eq/L bed, $q_0$ = 0). Feed = **0.05 N Na⁺** at $Q = 1.0$ mL/s. Bed porosity = 0.4. Estimate **breakthrough time**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Bed Volume**

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \frac{\pi}{4}(2.5)^2(30) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 187.5 = 147.3 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.147 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Breakthrough Equation**

$$t_b = \frac{V(q_{eq} - q_0)}{C_0 Q} - \frac{\epsilon V}{Q}$$

Plug in:

$$t_b = \frac{0.147 \text{ L} \times (1.8 - 0) \text{ eq/L}}{0.05 \text{ eq/L} \times 0.001 \text{ L/s}} - \frac{0.4 \times 0.147}{0.001}$$

$$t_b = \frac{0.2646}{5 \times 10^{-5}} - 58.8$$

$$t_b = 5292 - 58.8 = \boxed{5233 \text{ s} \approx 87.2 \text{ min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The first term is the **stoichiometric exchange capacity time**, the second is a **dead-time correction** for the liquid hold-up inside the porous bed. Ignoring $\epsilon$ would **overestimate** breakthrough by ~2.3%.

---

## 6.5 Worked Example 5 — Supersaturation & Nucleation Strategy

> **Problem:** For a target protein, $C^*$ = 2 mg/mL. Current concentration in the precipitator is **C = 8 mg/mL**. Calculate (a) supersaturation $S$, (b) classify the precipitation regime, and (c) suggest the engineering action.

### Solution

$$S = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*} = \frac{8 - 2}{2} = 3.0$$

> Regime: **S > 1 → Labile → Nucleation-dominant**.

> Action: Since $S$ is large, the precipitate will be **fine, amorphous, and impure**. Either (i) **dilute** the feed to bring S into the metastable range (0 < S < 1), or (ii) **add precipitant slowly** with intense mixing to avoid localized high S.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# VII. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 7.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bioseparation Downstream Train (Module 2 Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Crude Fermentation Broth] --> stepA[Cell Removal - Centrifugation or Filtration]
    stepA --> stepB[Primary Isolation - Module 2 Focus]
    stepB --> stepC[Polishing and Formulation]
    stepC --> outA[Pure Bioproduct]

    subgraph mod2A[Module 2 Isolation Toolbox]
        stepD[Ion Exchange Resin Column]
        stepE[Aqueous Two Phase System ATPS Mixer Settler]
        stepF[Precipitation Reactor with Salt or Solvent]
    end

    stepB -.uses.-> stepD
    stepB -.uses.-> stepE
    stepB -.uses.-> stepF
```

> **Engineering interpretation:** The block diagram shows how the three Module 2 techniques (ion-exchange, ATPS, precipitation) sit between primary cell-removal and final polishing steps. They are **interchangeable** depending on feed characteristics (ionic vs. hydrophobic, dilute vs. concentrated, labile vs. robust product).

---

## 7.2 Ion-Exchange Column Process Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Feed Tank] --> node2[Pump]
    node2 --> node3[Resin Column - Loaded Form]
    node3 --> node4[Breakthrough Detector]
    node4 -->|below 5 percent target exit| node5[Continue Loading]
    node4 -->|above 5 percent target exit| node6[Stop Loading - Switch to Wash]
    node6 --> node7[Wash with Buffer - 2 Bed Volumes]
    node7 --> node8[Elution with Regenerant - High Salt or pH Shift]
    node8 --> node9[Product Collection Vessel]
    node8 --> node10[Regenerant Reservoir]
    node10 -->|back to column| node3
```

---

## 7.3 ATPS Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[Crude Cell Lysate] --> mixA[Mixer Vessel]
    polyA[PEG Solution] --> mixA
    polyB[Dextran or Salt Solution] --> mixA
    mixA --> sepA[Static or Centrifugal Settler]
    sepA --> topA[Top Phase - PEG Rich - Light]
    sepA --> botA[Bottom Phase - Dextran Rich or Salt Rich - Heavy]
    topA --> an1[Analyze for Target Biomolecule]
    botA --> an1
    an1 --> rec1[Recover Target from Preferred Phase]
    rec1 --> polA[Optional Back Extraction or Polishing]
```

---

## 7.4 Precipitation Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedB[Clarified Broth with Target Protein] --> cond1[pH and Temperature Adjustment]
    cond1 --> reactB[Precipitation Reactor with Stirrer]
    saltB[(NH4)2SO4 Stock Solution] --> reactB
    reactB --> agedB[Aging Tank - 2 to 12 hours]
    agedB --> sepB[Centrifuge or Filtration Unit]
    sepB --> cakeB[Pellet or Cake - Target Protein]
    sepB --> supB[Supernatant - Waste or Recovery Stream]
    cakeB --> resolB[Resolubilization in Working Buffer]
    resolB --> polB[Downstream Polishing]
```

---

## 7.5 Selectivity Decision Map for Module 2 Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart{What is the main physicochemical property of your target?}
    qStart -->|Charged - small molecule or protein| qIE[Use Ion Exchange]
    qStart -->|Hydrophobic or large fragile biomolecule| qATPS[Use ATPS]
    qStart -->|High volume - bulk - low purity requirement| qPREC[Use Precipitation]

    qIE --> qIEsub{Feed is ionic and resin compatible?}
    qIEsub -->|Yes| qIERun[Selectivity K greater than 1 - Strong capture]
    qIEsub -->|No| qIEregen[Pre-treat with Diafiltration]

    qATPS --> qATPSsub{Polymer compatible with product stability?}
    qATPSsub -->|Yes| qATPSrun[Pick PEG MW and Salt Type for K target]
    qATPSsub -->|No| qATPSalt[Switch to ATPS with Salt only - Phosphate or Citrate]

    qPREC --> qPRECsub{Is high purity needed?}
    qPRECsub -->|Yes - one step| qPRECchain[Chain with IEX or ATPS downstream]
    qPRECsub -->|No - just concentration| qPRECdone[Standalone - Cohn process style]
```

---

## 7.6 Hofmeister / Selectivity Series Visualization (for Ion-Exchange Memory)

```
Cation Selectivity Increasing  →

Li+  <  H+  <  Na+  <  K+  <  Rb+  <  Cs+
            and
Mg2+  <  Ca2+  <  Sr2+  <  Ba2+
            and
Al3+  >  higher valences

(Strong Acid Cation Resin Pattern)
```

```
Anion Selectivity Increasing  →

F-  <  Cl-  <  Br-  <  NO3-  <  I-  <  ClO4-
            and
OH-  <  Chromate  <  Citrate  <  PO4 3-

(Strong Base Anion Resin Pattern)
```

# VIII. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 8.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> *"Define ion-exchange capacity. A strong acid cation resin in H⁺ form has 4.5 meq/g dry weight. What is the theoretical maximum Na⁺ uptake of 100 g of this resin?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ion-exchange capacity is the **total number of exchangeable counter-ions per unit mass or volume of resin**, expressed in eq/g (dry) or eq/L (wet bed). It is determined by the **concentration of fixed ionic groups** on the matrix.

Theoretical maximum Na⁺ uptake = (capacity) × (mass) = $4.5 \text{ meq/g} \times 100 \text{ g} = 450 \text{ meq} = 0.450 \text{ eq}$. 

This corresponds to $\frac{0.450}{1} = 0.450$ mol of Na⁺, i.e., $\boxed{10.35 \text{ g Na}^+}$.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> *"List three advantages of ATPS over conventional organic-aqueous liquid-liquid extraction."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Biocompatibility** — Both phases are >70% water; no harsh organic-aqueous interface, so proteins retain native folding and activity.
2. **Low interfacial tension** (~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻² mN/m) → gentle mixing → minimal denaturation.
3. **Easy scale-up & green process** — Avoids toxic solvents (e.g., butanol, chloroform), making it **environmentally sustainable** and acceptable for pharmaceutical regulatory filings.

*(Acceptable extras: low cost, low energy, continuous operation possible, ease of integrating chromatography.)*

---

## 8.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Ion-Exchange Focused

> **Question A (Part-A):** *(7 marks)*
> Discuss the **physical description, separation mechanisms, and equipment** used in **ion-exchange processes** for protein purification. Include a labelled schematic of a packed-bed ion-exchange column.

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Physical description** (2 marks):
   - Cross-linked polymer (PS-DVB) matrix.
   - Fixed ionic groups (–SO₃⁻ for SAC, –N⁺(CH₃)₃ for SBA).
   - Bead form; size 50–500 μm; gel vs. macroporous structures.

2. **Separation mechanism** (3 marks):
   - Coulombic attraction between fixed groups and counter-ions.
   - Stoichiometric exchange governed by selectivity coefficient $K_A^B$.
   - Hofmeister selectivity series; secondary interactions (van der Waals, hydrophobic).

3. **Equipment** (2 marks):
   - Packed bed columns (lab to industrial).
   - Stirred-tank batch contactors.
   - Fluidised-bed and simulated moving bed (SMB) for continuous operation.

---

> **Question A (Part-B):** *(7 marks)*
> A **cation-exchange column** of 1.0 L bed volume (capacity 1.6 eq/L) is loaded with a feed containing **0.20 M Na⁺** at a flow rate of **2.0 L/min**. The bed porosity is 0.4. Estimate the **breakthrough time** if the breakthrough concentration is defined at **5% of inlet Na⁺ concentration**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*(Assume $q_0 \approx 0$ and $q_{eq} \approx 1.6$ eq/L bed since capacity is given per bed volume.)*

$$t_b \approx \frac{V (q_{eq} - q_0)}{C_0 \cdot Q} - \frac{\epsilon V}{Q}$$

$$t_b = \frac{1.0 \text{ L} \times 1.6 \text{ eq/L}}{0.20 \text{ eq/L} \times 2.0 \text{ L/min}} - \frac{0.4 \times 1.0}{2.0}$$

$$t_b = \frac{1.6}{0.4} - 0.2 = 4.0 - 0.2 = \boxed{3.8 \text{ min}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** After ~3.8 minutes, the effluent Na⁺ concentration rises sharply — the resin must be switched to the **wash** and **elution** steps to recover captured proteins and regenerate the bed.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — ATPS + Precipitation Combined

> **Question B (Part-A):** *(7 marks)*
> Describe the **theory, design and applications of aqueous two-phase separations**. Explain how the partition coefficient (K) and volume ratio (R) affect recovery and purification factor.

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Theory** (2 marks):
   - Binodal curve and tie-lines.
   - Flory-Huggins lattice model; $\chi$ parameter.
   - Driving forces: hydrophobic, electrostatic, biospecific.

2. **Design** (3 marks):
   - Selection of polymers (PEG MW, dextran vs. salts).
   - Phase diagram construction; tie-line length selection.
   - Mixer-settler or column contactor design.

3. **Applications** (2 marks):
   - mAb purification, plasmid DNA recovery, enzyme extraction, cell organelle isolation.

---

> **Question B (Part-B):** *(7 marks)*
> An **enzyme** is extracted in an ATPS with **K = 12** and **volume ratio R = 0.5**. Determine:
> (a) The recovery in the top phase.
> (b) The minimum **R** required to achieve **95% recovery**.
> (c) Comment on the selectivity (purification factor) if a contaminant has K = 0.8.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(a) Recovery at R = 0.5:**

$$Y_T = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{R K}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{0.5 \times 12}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1/6} = \frac{1}{7/6} = \boxed{0.857 \text{ or } 85.7\%}$$

**(b) Minimum R for 95% recovery:**

Set $Y_T = 0.95$:

$$0.95 = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{R K}}$$

$$1 + \frac{1}{R K} = \frac{1}{0.95} = 1.0526$$

$$\frac{1}{R K} = 0.0526 \Rightarrow R = \frac{1}{12 \times 0.0526} = \frac{1}{0.6316} = \boxed{1.583}$$

→ Requires a **top phase ≥ 1.58× the bottom phase volume** — operationally feasible but increases reagent cost.

**(c) Selectivity / Purification Factor:**

Selectivity β = $K_{enzyme}/K_{contaminant} = 12/0.8 = 15.0$ → excellent discrimination; the contaminant remains in the bottom phase while the enzyme partitions to the top phase, giving a **purification factor of ~15×**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Module 2**
> 1. **Mixing up selectivity ($K_A^B$) with partition coefficient (K)** — these belong to **different techniques**. Selectivity is for ion-exchange; K (no subscript) is for ATPS. Writing one where the other is asked = **−1 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\epsilon V/Q$ dead-time correction** in breakthrough calculations — examiners specifically check this — **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing S (supersaturation, dimensionless) with S (solubility, mg/mL)** in precipitation — re-define every symbol the **first time** it appears.
> 4. **Ignoring valence balance** — writing R–A + B⁺ instead of R₂–A + B²⁺ for divalent exchange → **stoichiometry wrong → full marks lost**.
> 5. **Not specifying units** (eq, meq, mol) in selectivity equations → **−0.5 mark per occurrence**.
> 6. **In ATPS, omitting the "aqueous" qualifier** — calling it a simple liquid-liquid extraction → **partial credit only**.

---

## 8.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

### 🔹 Ion-Exchange

- Resin = insoluble polymer with **fixed ionic groups**; counter-ions are **mobile and exchangeable**.
- Driving force = **Coulombic attraction**; secondary forces = hydrophobic, H-bonding.
- Selectivity follows **Hofmeister series** for simple ions; **proteins** follow more complex rules (charge, hydrophobicity, size).
- **Equipment:** Packed-bed column (most common), stirred-tank batch, fluidised bed, simulated moving bed (SMB).
- **Regeneration:** Concentrated counter-ion solution or pH shift; cycle = load → wash → elute → regenerate → re-equilibrate.
- Capacity units: **meq/g dry, eq/L wet, eq/mL bed**.

### 🔹 Aqueous Two-Phase Separation (ATPS)

- Both phases contain **>70% water** — gentle, biocompatible.
- Common systems: **PEG/dextran**, **PEG/salt** (phosphate, citrate, sulfate).
- Phase diagrams show **binodal curve** + **tie-lines**.
- Partition coefficient **K = $C_T/C_B$** → ideally ≫1 or ≪1.
- Recovery $Y_T = \dfrac{1}{1+\frac{1}{RK}}$; purification factor ∝ β = $K_{target}/K_{contam}$.
- Applications: mAbs, plasmid DNA, virus-like particles, organelle isolation.

### 🔹 Precipitation

- **Cohn Equation:** $\log S = \log S_0 - K_S I$ — straight-line relationship between log-solubility and ionic strength.
- **Methods (6 canonical):** salting-out, isoelectric, organic solvent, polyelectrolyte, metal-ion, affinity.
- **(NH₄)₂SO₄** is the most-used salt; **ethanol** is the classical Cohn-fractionation solvent.
- Nucleation vs. growth controlled by **supersaturation S = (C − C*)/C***.
- Applications: **blood plasma fractionation**, **industrial enzyme recovery**, **early-stage concentration** in antibiotic fermentations.

### 🔹 Cross-Cutting Examination Strategy

1. **Always** draw a block diagram when asked about equipment/process flow (carries **2 marks** in most 14-mark answers).
2. **Always** state the driving force at the beginning of the answer — this is the first thing the examiner scans for.
3. **Always** provide one **industrial application** example — examiners reward real-world linkage.
4. **Always** state units explicitly in equations.
5. **Always** mention **regeneration / recyclability** for ion-exchange — sustainability is a 2024-scheme emphasis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Home Pearl**
> The three Module 2 techniques (**IEX, ATPS, precipitation**) are **complementary, not competing**. Industrial bioprocesses often **chain** them: e.g., *cell removal → ATPS (clarification + concentration) → IEX (polishing) → precipitation (final formulation)*. Recognize this synergy in design problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion-Exchange in Purification-Ion-Exchange Equilibria of Biochemicals, Chromatographic Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT865 - ADVANCED BIO SEPARATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion-Exchange in Purification-Ion-Exchange Equilibria of Biochemicals, Chromatographic Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ion-Exchange: Formal Definition

**Ion-exchange** is a stoichiometric, reversible exchange of ions between a solid *ion exchanger* (resin or matrix) and an electrolyte solution in which the exchanger is not structurally altered. In biochemical separations, ion-exchange operates as a **chromatographic adsorption–desorption process** in which charged biomolecules (proteins, amino acids, nucleotides, antibiotics) compete with counter-ions already bound to a charged stationary phase.

The two principal classes are:

- **Cation Exchangers** – Carry fixed negatively charged groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻, –COO⁻). They bind positively charged solutes (cations, basic proteins at pH < pI).
- **Anion Exchangers** – Carry fixed positively charged groups (e.g., –N(CH₃)₃⁺). They bind negatively charged solutes (anions, acidic proteins at pH > pI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ion-exchange in biochemical purification is treated as an **equilibrium-staged sorption** operation governed by the **law of mass action** and constrained by **Donnan membrane equilibrium** for polyelectrolytes such as proteins.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Magnet Trap"

Imagine a tray filled with thousands of tiny magnetic beads, each pre-loaded with weakly held "trading tokens" (counter-ions such as Na⁺ or Cl⁻). When a charged molecule (say, a positively charged lysozyme protein) flows into the column, it has a much stronger "magnetic attraction" to the beads than the token. So the protein docks onto the bead and pushes the token off into the stream. Later, when you want your protein back, you simply flood the column with a **strong salt solution (eluent)** — this raises the concentration of free trading tokens so massively that statistically, your protein gets outbid and gets washed off the bead.

This "auction" between your target molecule and the salt ions for the charged bead is, in mathematical form, the **Ion-Exchange Equilibrium**.

## 1.3 Chromatographic Processes — Intuitive View

A **chromatographic process** is a physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two phases:
- A **stationary phase** (fixed in a column or on a planar surface)
- A **mobile phase** (a fluid – gas or liquid – that percolates over/through the stationary phase)

Differential migration of solutes along the column arises from differences in their **distribution (partition) coefficients** between the two phases. Ion-exchange chromatography (IEX) is one of the **five principal modes**:

| Mode | Stationary Phase Character | Solute Character |
|------|---------------------------|------------------|
| Ion-exchange (IEX) | Charged | Oppositely charged |
| Reverse-phase (RPC) | Hydrophobic C18/C8 | Hydrophobic solutes |
| Size-exclusion (SEC) | Porous beads | Different hydrodynamic radii |
| Affinity (AC) | Ligand (e.g., Ni²⁺-NTA) | His-tag, antibody |
| Hydrophobic-interaction (HIC) | Mild hydrophobic | Surface hydrophobic patches |

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational distinction:** In *ion-exchange chromatography* the driving force is **electrostatic**; in *affinity chromatography* it is **biospecific**; in *size exclusion* it is purely **entropic (size-based)**.

## 1.4 The Ion-Exchange Material (Resin) — Key Physical Constants

- **Capacity (Q):** Reported in **meq/g dry resin** or **meq/mL wet resin**. Typical strong cation resin: **4.5–5.0 meq/g**; DEAE-cellulose: **0.9–1.0 meq/g**.
- **Cross-linkage (% DVB):** Standard polystyrene-DVB resin: **8 % w/w**.
- **Bead diameter:** 50–500 µm for process columns; 5–10 µm for analytical HPLC.
- **Swelling factor:** Volume change between H⁺ and Na⁺ forms: ≈ 7 % for 8 % DVB resin.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of an Ion-Exchange Cycle

A complete ion-exchange process for biomolecule purification proceeds through **five discrete stages**:

1. **Equilibration** – The column is washed with a low-ionic-strength *start buffer* (e.g., 20 mM phosphate, pH 6.5) so that the resin's counter-ions are exchanged to the running form (e.g., Na⁺ for CM-Sepharose).
2. **Loading (Sorption)** – The clarified, desalted feedstock is passed through the column. Charged target molecules displace counter-ions and bind to the fixed charges of the resin.
3. **Washing** – A buffer of the same pH but slightly higher salt (e.g., 50 mM NaCl) removes unbound, weakly retained contaminants and restores the baseline UV trace.
4. **Elution (Desorption)** – Either (a) a *salt gradient* (0 → 1 M NaCl) or (b) a *pH gradient* (or both) progressively weakens electrostatic binding, releasing the target.
5. **Regeneration & Cleaning-in-Place (CIP)** – Strong acid/base washes restore the column and remove fouling species.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two elution modes work:** Increasing salt **shields** the fixed charge (reduces effective potential); changing pH **neutralizes** the target protein's charge (reduces its z). At the protein's **isoelectric point (pI)**, its net charge = 0 → electrostatic binding → 0 → elution.

## 2.2 Ion-Exchange Equilibria — Mass Action Formalism

For the simplest binary exchange between a monovalent counter-ion **B⁺** on the resin and a target cation **A⁺** in solution:

$$
A^+_{(aq)} + B^+_{(R)} \rightleftharpoons A^+_{(R)} + B^+_{(aq)}
$$

Applying the law of mass action at equilibrium:

$$
K_{AB} \;=\; \frac{[A^+]_R \cdot [B^+]}{[B^+]_R \cdot [A^+]}
$$

where:
- $K_{AB}$ = **selectivity (equilibrium) coefficient** (dimensionless)
- $[\cdot]_R$ = concentration in the resin phase (eq/L of wet resin)
- $[\cdot]$ = concentration in the liquid phase (eq/L)

For a binary exchange between two competing ions of **different valences** $z_A$ and $z_B$, the equilibrium is written in **equivalent** (not molar) terms to preserve the correct stoichiometry:

$$
z_B \, A^{z_A+} + z_A \, B^{z_B+}_{(R)} \rightleftharpoons z_B \, A^{z_A+}_{(R)} + z_A \, B^{z_B+}
$$

$$
K_{AB} \;=\; \frac{[A]^{z_B}_R \cdot [B]^{z_A}}{[B]^{z_A}_R \cdot [A]^{z_B}}
$$

## 2.3 Distribution Coefficient ($K_d$) and Linear Driving Force

The **distribution coefficient** (often denoted $\lambda$ or $K_d$) is the dimensionless ratio of solute concentration in the resin phase to that in the liquid phase at equilibrium:

$$
K_d \;=\; \frac{q_A}{c_A} \;=\; \frac{\text{amount of A on resin (eq/kg)}}{\text{concentration of A in solution (eq/L)}}
$$

A high $K_d$ means strong retention; $K_d = 1$ means equal preference between phases. In process chromatography, the *retention factor* $k'$ is related:

$$
k' \;=\; \frac{t_R - t_0}{t_0} \;=\; \frac{K_d \cdot \rho_s (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon}
$$

where $\rho_s$ = resin bulk density and $\varepsilon$ = column void fraction.

## 2.4 Donnan Equilibrium — Why Proteins Behave Differently

When the exchanging solute is a **polyelectrolyte (protein, DNA, poly-amino acid)**, the resin's fixed charges create an electrostatic potential ($\psi_D$, the **Donnan potential**). The ion product inside and outside the resin phase must be equal for each salt type:

$$
(c_{M^+} \cdot c_{X^-}})_{\text{inside resin}} \;=\; (c_{M^+} \cdot c_{X^-}})_{\text{outside}}
$$

For a cation exchanger with fixed anionic groups $X^-_R$ at concentration $Q$ and a 1:1 electrolyte $M^+X^-$:

$$
c_{M^+,\, \text{inside}} \;=\; c_{M^+,\, \text{outside}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{F\psi_D}{RT}\right)
$$

This Donnan exclusion is why **proteins, which are themselves polyelectrolytes**, are sometimes *repelled* from the resin even when nominally oppositely charged — the high local salt concentration near fixed charges screens the binding.

## 2.5 Chromatographic Process Performance Metrics

### 2.5.1 Plate Theory (Number of Theoretical Plates)

$$
N \;=\; 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_b}\right)^2 \;=\; 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2
$$

where $t_R$ = retention time, $w_b$ = baseline peak width, $w_{1/2}$ = half-height width.

**Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP):**

$$
H \;=\; \frac{L}{N}
$$

### 2.5.2 Resolution Equation

The **Purnell Resolution Equation** governs the ability to separate two adjacent peaks:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'}
$$

where $\alpha$ = selectivity factor, $k'$ = retention factor of the second eluting peak. The three bracketed terms correspond, respectively, to **efficiency**, **selectivity**, and **retention**.

### 2.5.3 Van Deemter Equation (Band Broadening)

$$
H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u
$$

| Term | Physical Cause | Practical Cure |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| $A$ (Eddy diffusion) | Flow-path dispersion | Smaller, uniform beads |
| $B/u$ (Longitudinal diffusion) | Molecular diffusion along axis | Increase linear velocity |
| $C \cdot u$ (Mass-transfer resistance) | Slow equilibration between phases | Thin films, small beads |

### 2.5.4 Breakthrough Curve & Dynamic Capacity

For a column of length $L$ and interstitial velocity $u$, the breakthrough time $t_B$ and dynamic (10 %) capacity are related to the *number of transfer units* NTU:

$$
\text{NTU} \;=\; \int_{c_{in}}^{c_{out}} \frac{dc}{c^* - c} \;=\; \frac{K_f \cdot a \cdot L}{u}
$$

where $K_f$ = film mass-transfer coefficient (m/s), $a$ = specific surface area (m²/m³), and $c^*$ = equilibrium liquid concentration.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *high-frequency* in KTU ESE papers. Memorize both the equation and the **physical meaning of each variable**.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $K_{AB} = (q_A \, c_B) / (q_B \, c_A)$ | Binary selectivity coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $K_d = q_A / c_A$ | Linear distribution coefficient | L/kg resin |
| 3 | $k' = (t_R - t_0)/t_0$ | Retention factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$ | Selectivity factor (always $>1$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot ((\alpha-1)/\alpha) \cdot (k'/(1+k'))$ | Purnell resolution | dimensionless |
| 6 | $N = 16 (t_R/w_b)^2$ | Plate count | dimensionless |
| 7 | $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ | Van Deemter plate-height | m |
| 8 | $HETP = L/N$ | Plate height | m |
| 9 | $c_{in,\,i} \cdot c_{in,\,j} = c_{out,\,i} \cdot c_{out,\,j}$ | Donnan ion-product equality | (mol/L)² |
| 10 | $\text{NTU} = K_f a L / u$ | Mass-transfer units | dimensionless |
| 11 | $Q = \int_0^{V_B} (c_0 - c)\,dV / m_{resin}$ | Dynamic capacity (breakthrough) | eq/kg |
| 12 | $D_{ax} = u L / \text{Pe}$ | Axial dispersion coefficient | m²/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error trap:** In the selectivity coefficient formula, the stoichiometry rule for non-equal valences is **frequently forgotten** in KTU valuations. Always check valences before writing $K_{AB}$.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of IEX |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Pharmaceutical biotech** | Capture step for monoclonal antibodies (Protein A often replaced by cation-exchange in polishing). |
| **Water treatment** | Hardness removal: $Ca^{2+}_{aq} + 2\,Na^+_R \rightarrow Ca^{2+}_R + 2\,Na^+_{aq}$. |
| **Sugar refining** | Demineralization of cane juice using strong cation/anion pairs. |
| **Hydrometallurgy** | Selective recovery of $UO_2^{2+}$, $Cu^{2+}$, $RE^{3+}$ from leachates. |
| **Analytical HPLC** | Amino-acid analysis on sulfonated polystyrene with citrate buffers. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Selectivity Coefficient from Mass Action

Consider the general exchange:

$$
z_B\, A^{z_A+} \,+\, z_A\, B^{z_B+}_R \;\rightleftharpoons\; z_B\, A^{z_A+}_R \,+\, z_A\, B^{z_B+}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the forward / reverse rate expressions:**

$$
r_f \;=\; k_f \, [A^{z_A+}]^{z_B} \, [B^{z_B+}_R]^{z_A}
$$

$$
r_r \;=\; k_r \, [A^{z_A+}_R]^{z_B} \, [B^{z_B+}]^{z_A}
$$

**Step 2 — At equilibrium, $r_f = r_r$:**

$$
k_f \, [A]^{z_B} \, [B_R]^{z_A} \;=\; k_r \, [A_R]^{z_B} \, [B]^{z_A}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the equilibrium (selectivity) constant as $K_{AB} \equiv k_f/k_r$:**

$$
K_{AB} \;=\; \frac{[A_R]^{z_B}\,[B]^{z_A}}{[A]^{z_B}\,[B_R]^{z_A}}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply and isolate for the case $z_A = z_B = 1$ (binary monovalent exchange):**

$$
K_{AB} \;=\; \frac{[A]_R \, [B]}{[A] \, [B]_R}
$$

This is the **binary selectivity coefficient** $K_{AB}$. A value of $K_{AB} > 1$ means that ion $A$ is preferentially held by the resin over ion $B$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Donnan Potential

A cation-exchange resin carries fixed anionic groups at concentration $Q$ (eq/L). The mobile counter-ion is $M^+$ and co-ion is $X^-$, both at concentration $c$ in the bulk.

**Step 1 — Electrical neutrality inside the resin phase:**

$$
c_{M^+,\, \text{in}} \;=\; Q \;+\; c_{X^-,\, \text{in}}
$$

**Step 2 — Electrochemical equilibrium for each ion (Boltzmann distribution):**

$$
c_{M^+,\, \text{in}} \;=\; c_{M^+,\, \text{out}} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{F\psi_D}{RT}\right)
$$

$$
c_{X^-,\, \text{in}} \;=\; c_{X^-,\, \text{out}} \, \exp\!\left(+\frac{F\psi_D}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the two Boltzmann equations:**

$$
c_{M^+,\, \text{in}} \cdot c_{X^-,\, \text{in}} \;=\; c_{M^+,\, \text{out}} \cdot c_{X^-,\, \text{out}}
$$

This is the famous **Donnan ion-product equality**.

**Step 4 — Solve for the Donnan potential $\psi_D$:**

Let $c_+ = c_{M^+,\text{out}}$ and $c_- = c_{X^-,\text{out}}$. Then:

$$
\psi_D \;=\; \frac{RT}{F} \sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Q}{2c}\right)
$$

This shows that as the **bulk salt concentration $c$** increases, $\psi_D$ decreases — the resin's fixed charge is increasingly screened. **Operational implication:** above ~0.5 M salt, ion-exchange binding effectively vanishes, hence the choice of salt gradients in elution.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Van Deemter Equation

**Step 1 — Eddy diffusion term ($A$):**

Different flow paths through the packed bed have different lengths. The variance contribution is $\sigma_A^2 = 2\lambda_1 d_p L$, giving:

$$
A \;=\; 2\lambda_1 d_p
$$

with $\lambda_1$ ≈ 0.5–1.0.

**Step 2 — Longitudinal molecular diffusion ($B/u$):**

Random thermal motion along the flow axis. Variance $\sigma_B^2 = 2 D_m L / u$. Therefore:

$$
B \;=\; 2 \gamma_2 D_m
$$

**Step 3 — Mass-transfer resistance ($C \cdot u$):**

Finite time for solute to move between mobile and stationary phases. Two contributions: film resistance (mobile-side) and pore-diffusion resistance (stationary-side):

$$
C \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega} \left(\frac{k'}{1+k'}\right)^2 \frac{d_p^2}{D_s} \;+\; \text{(film term)}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of variances (assumption of independence):**

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{total}}}{L} \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Breakthrough Curve (Thomas Solution)

Consider an isothermal, plug-flow column of length $L$ with linear isotherm $q = K_d c$. Material balance on a slice $dz$ over time $dt$:

$$
\varepsilon \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} \;+\; u \frac{\partial c}{\partial z} \;+\; (1-\varepsilon)\rho_s \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} \;=\; 0
$$

With $\partial q/\partial t = K_d \, \partial c/\partial t$, this collapses to:

$$
\left[\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_s K_d\right] \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} \;+\; u \frac{\partial c}{\partial z} \;=\; 0
$$

The combined bracketed term is the **total (mobile + stationary) solute per unit volume**, defining the **retardation factor** $R_f$:

$$
R_f \;=\; 1 + \frac{(1-\varepsilon)\rho_s K_d}{\varepsilon} \;=\; 1 + k'
$$

The travelling-wave (Thomas) solution for $c(0, t) = c_0$ and $c(z, 0) = 0$ is:

$$
\frac{c(z,t)}{c_0} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \left\{1 + \text{erf}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\text{Pe}}\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_R}\right)\right]\right\}
$$

with the **Peclet number** $\text{Pe} = u L / D_{ax}$ and **stoichiometric time** $t_R = R_f \, z/u$.

**Operational insight:** At $c/c_0 = 0.1$ the breakthrough point is reached. The 50 %-breakthrough corresponds to $t = t_R$ (the ideal stoichiometric time).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-pattern 14-mark)

**Problem:** A cation-exchange resin column (50 mL bed, void fraction $\varepsilon = 0.4$, bulk density $\rho_s = 1.2$ g/mL) is loaded with a feedstock containing **lysozyme** ($M = 14\,300$ Da, $z = +8$ at pH 7) at $c_0 = 2$ mg/mL in 20 mM phosphate buffer. The distribution coefficient $K_d$ has been measured as **120 mL/g**. Determine (a) the retention factor $k'$, (b) the breakthrough volume $V_B$ at 10 % breakthrough, (c) the dynamic capacity.

**Solution:**

**(a) Retention factor**

$$
k' \;=\; \frac{K_d \, \rho_s \, (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{120 \times 1.2 \times 0.6}{0.4} \;=\; \frac{86.4}{0.4} \;=\; 216
$$

**(b) Stoichiometric (50 %) breakthrough volume**

$$
t_R \;=\; \frac{L}{u} (1 + k') \quad \text{and} \quad V_R \;=\; Q \, t_R
$$

For a column of 50 mL wet volume with superficial velocity $u = 30$ cm/h and $L = 15$ cm, $t_R = (15/30)\times(1+216) = 0.5 \times 217 = 108.5$ h → $V_R = 50 \times 108.5 = 5425$ mL ≈ **5.4 L**.

**(c) Dynamic (10 %) breakthrough volume**

For a sharp front (Pe $\gg$ 1), $V_B \approx 0.9 V_R$:

$$
V_B \;\approx\; 0.9 \times 5.4 \;\approx\; 4.9 \text{ L}
$$

**Dynamic capacity of the bed:**

$$
Q_{B} \;=\; \frac{V_B \cdot c_0}{V_{\text{resin}}} \;=\; \frac{4.9 \text{ L} \times 2 \text{ mg/mL}}{50 \text{ mL}} \;=\; \frac{9800}{50} \;\approx\; 196 \text{ mg lysozyme per mL resin}
$$

This is consistent with typical cation-exchange capture of lysozyme reported in literature (~150–250 mg/mL on SP-Sepharose FF).

```mermaid
    nodeSTRONG --> nodeANS[Quaternary Amine NCH3 3 positive for anions]
    nodeWEAK[Weak Ion Exchangers pH dependent partially ionized] --> nodeCATW[Carboxymethyl COO negative pKa around 4]
    nodeWEAK --> nodeANW[Diethylaminoethyl DEAE positive pKa around 9.5]
    nodeCATS --> nodeCHROM1[IEX Column Process]
    nodeANS --> nodeCHROM1
    nodeCATW --> nodeCHROM1
    nodeANW --> nodeCHROM1

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the law of mass action as applied to a binary ion-exchange equilibrium between two monovalent ions. Define the selectivity coefficient.**

**Model Answer:**

For the exchange $A^+_{(aq)} + B^+_{(R)} \rightleftharpoons A^+_{(R)} + B^+_{(aq)}$, the law of mass action gives the **binary selectivity coefficient**:

$$
K_{AB} \;=\; \frac{[A^+]_R\,[B^+]}{[B^+]_R\,[A^+]}
$$

- $K_{AB} > 1$ : the resin preferentially sorbs ion $A$.
- $K_{AB} < 1$ : the resin preferentially sorbs ion $B$.
- $K_{AB} = 1$ : no preference; ideal Donnan exchanger.

**Practical examples:** Strong-acid cation resin prefers $Ca^{2+}$ over $Na^+$ ($K = 1.8$ at low loading) — the basis of water softening. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between strong and weak ion exchangers. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Strong Ion Exchanger | Weak Ion Exchanger |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Functional group | Sulfonic acid (–SO₃H); quaternary amine (–N(CH₃)₃⁺) | Carboxymethyl (–COOH); DEAE (–N(C₂H₅)₂H⁺) |
| pH dependence | Ionized over the entire pH range | Ionized only within a narrow pH window |
| Regeneration ease | Harder (large excess of regenerant) | Easy (stoichiometric) |
| Typical use | Continuous demineralization | Protein chromatography (gentle elution) |
| Example | Dowex 50 (cation), Amberlite IRA-400 (anion) | CM-Sepharose (cation), DEAE-Cellulose (anion) |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **Donnan equilibrium expression** for a cation-exchange resin in contact with a 1:1 electrolyte. Explain how the Donnan potential varies with (i) fixed-charge density $Q$ and (ii) external salt concentration $c$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the working of an **ion-exchange chromatography column** for the purification of an enzyme from a crude cell extract. Discuss the role of buffer pH, ionic strength, and resin type. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation of the Donnan equilibrium (7 marks):**

Inside the cation exchanger, electroneutrality demands:

$$
c_{M^+,\,\text{in}} \;=\; Q + c_{X^-,\,\text{in}}
$$

For each ion the electrochemical potential equilibrium yields the Boltzmann factor:

$$
c_{M^+,\,\text{in}} = c_{M^+,\,\text{out}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{F\psi_D}{RT}\right), \quad c_{X^-,\,\text{in}} = c_{X^-,\,\text{out}} \exp\!\left(+\frac{F\psi_D}{RT}\right)
$$

Multiplying cancels $\psi_D$:

$$
c_{M^+,\,\text{in}}\,c_{X^-,\,\text{in}} \;=\; c_{M^+,\,\text{out}}\,c_{X^-,\,\text{out}}
$$

Setting both bulk concentrations to $c$ (a 1:1 electrolyte at concentration $c$):

$$
\psi_D \;=\; \frac{RT}{F}\,\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Q}{2c}\right)
$$

**Variation with $Q$:** $\psi_D$ increases monotonically with $Q$ — a more strongly charged resin exerts a larger electrostatic pull on counter-ions.

**Variation with $c$:** $\psi_D$ decreases as $c$ rises — high external salt screens the fixed charges. When $c \gg Q$, $\psi_D \to 0$ and the resin behaves as a Donnan-exclusion medium; ion-exchange capacity is effectively lost. *This is why elution is performed at high salt.*

**(b) Working of an IEX column (7 marks):**

A typical setup consists of:
1. **Packed column** — borosilicate glass with adjustable end-pieces; bed height 10–30 cm; bed volume 10–500 mL.
2. **Peristaltic pump / FPLC system** — delivers buffers at 50–500 cm/h linear velocity.
3. **Inlet mixer / gradient former** — for generating a continuous 0→1 M NaCl gradient.
4. **Outlet** — UV detector (280 nm) → conductivity meter → fraction collector.
5. **Buffer reservoir** — equilibration buffer (20 mM phosphate pH 6.5), elution buffer (20 mM phosphate + 1 M NaCl), regeneration buffer (1 M NaOH), CIP (0.5 M NaOH + 1 M NaCl).

**Procedure (see schematic in §4.1):**
1. Equilibrate with 5 column volumes (CV) of start buffer until pH and conductivity at outlet equal inlet.
2. Load clarified lysate at linear velocity ≤ 150 cm/h.
3. Wash with 3–5 CV of start buffer + 50 mM NaCl until UV returns to baseline.
4. Elute with a 10-CV linear gradient 0–1 M NaCl; collect 1-mL fractions.
5. Regenerate with 3 CV of 1 M NaOH, then re-equilibrate.

**Roles of key variables:**

- **pH:** Determines net charge of protein ($z_p$) and degree of ionization of weak ion exchangers. Rule: choose pH at least 1 unit away from protein's pI.
- **Ionic strength:** Governs $k'$ and resolution. Start low (≤ 20 mM) for binding, end high (≥ 500 mM) for elution.
- **Resin type:** Cation (SP, CM) for basic proteins; anion (Q, DEAE) for acidic proteins. Strong exchangers (SP, Q) tolerate aggressive CIP; weak ones (CM, DEAE) require milder conditions.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
