<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — DOWNSTREAM PROCESS ECONOMICS & SCALE-UP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Downstream Process Economics (DPE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Downstream Process Economics (DPE)** is the quantitative evaluation of the financial viability of all purification, separation, and formulation operations carried out after a bioproduct (e.g., recombinant protein, enzyme, vaccine) has been produced via fermentation or cell culture. It integrates **capital expenditure (CapEx)**, **operating expenditure (OpEx)**, **return on investment (ROI)**, and **cash flow dynamics** to determine whether a bioprocess can be commercialized profitably at industrial scale.

In simpler words, *DPE is the science of asking the question — "Will the money we spend to purify this protein ever come back as profit?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In recombinant biotechnology, downstream processing (DSP) typically accounts for **60–80 %** of the total production cost of a biopharmaceutical, while upstream fermentation accounts for only 20–40 %. Hence, mastering DSP economics is the single largest lever of profitability for a bioprocess engineer.

---

### 1.2 Commercialization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercialization** is the transition of a laboratory-scale or pilot-scale bioprocess into a full-scale, market-ready, GMP-compliant industrial manufacturing process. It involves technology transfer, regulatory approvals (FDA / EMA / DCGI), scale-up validation, and supply-chain readiness.

---

### 1.3 Scaling-Up

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling-Up** is the geometric and operational enlargement of a process from bench-scale (1–10 L fermenter) → pilot-scale (100–1000 L) → demonstration-scale (1–10 m³) → production-scale (50–250 m³), while maintaining critical process parameters such as product yield, purity, and quality.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Chain" Model

Imagine a chef who cooks a fantastic single portion of *Biryani* in her home kitchen (lab-scale). It tastes incredible.

- **Commercialization** = opening a restaurant chain that serves the *same* dish to 1000 customers every day, with standardized taste, hygiene, and pricing.
- **Scale-Up** = buying bigger vessels, larger stoves, industrial spice-grinders — but the **recipe (process recipe / SOP)** must remain identical.
- **Downstream Process Economics** = deciding whether the cost of the bigger kitchen, more chefs, electricity, gas, and spices still leaves you with a profit on each plate sold.
- **ROI** = if you invested ₹10 lakh on the bigger kitchen, how many months of biryani sales will it take to recover that ₹10 lakh?
- **Cash Flow** = tracking money *coming in* (customer payments) vs. money *going out* (salaries, gas bills, spice purchases) every month.
- **Sensitivity Analysis** = *"What if the price of basmati rice doubles — will I still be profitable? What if my chef gets sick and productivity drops 20 %?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **This analogy is used throughout KTU board valuations.** Replace "biryani" with "recombinant Human Insulin" and the analogy maps 1-to-1 to industrial biotechnology.

---

### 1.5 Case Study Anchor — Recombinant Human Insulin in *E. coli*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Flagship Case Study (KTU Dec 2023 / Jun 2024 pattern):**
> **Human Insulin** was the first recombinant DNA therapeutic approved by the FDA (Eli Lilly's **Humulin**, 1982). It is produced by inserting the human insulin gene into *Escherichia coli* K-12 strains. The pro-insulin is expressed as inclusion bodies, which are then:
> 1. Harvested by centrifugation,
> 2. Solubilized in denaturant (urea / guanidine-HCl),
> 3. Refolded by dilution/dialysis,
> 4. Purified by ion-exchange and reverse-phase chromatography,
> 5. Crystallized as **Zinc-Insulin hexamers**.

This case study is the canonical KTU example because it illustrates *every* downstream unit operation: **centrifugation → cell disruption → solubilization → refolding → multi-column chromatography → crystallization → lyophilization**.

---

### 1.6 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| Lang Factor (Fluid Processing) | $F_{lang}$ | **4.28** |
| Lang Factor (Solid Processing) | $F_{lang}$ | **3.10** |
| Plant availability | — | **330 days/year (90.4 % uptime)** |
| Operator wage (Indian industry avg.) | — | **₹ 35,000/month** |
| Insulin global market price | $P_{ins}$ | **₹ 250 / unit (≈ USD 3)** |
| Typical industrial bioreactor scale | $V_{BR}$ | **50–250 m³** |
| Working capital fraction | $f_{WC}$ | **10–20 % of FCI** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Process Economics

A biotech entrepreneur must answer three questions before commercializing a product:

1. **How much money do I need to build the plant?** → *Capital Cost Estimation*
2. **How much will it cost to run the plant every year?** → *Operating Cost Estimation*
3. **Will the profits justify the investment?** → *ROI / NPV / IRR / Cash Flow Analysis*

---

## 2.2 Pillar 1 — Capital Cost (CapEx) Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Cost = Total Capital Investment (TCI)** = Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) + Working Capital (WC).

### 2.2.1 The Lang Factor Method (Most common in KTU board exams)

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Origin:** Hans Lang (1947) observed that the ratio of total plant cost to delivered equipment cost is roughly constant for similar process types.

$$C_{FCI} = F_{lang} \times \sum C_{equipment}$$

Where:
- $C_{FCI}$ = Fixed Capital Investment
- $F_{lang}$ = Lang Factor (dimensionless)
- $\sum C_{equipment}$ = Total delivered cost of all major equipment items (USD)

| Process Type | Lang Factor $F_{lang}$ |
|---|---|
| Solid Processing (e.g., crystallization, drying) | **3.10** |
| Fluid Processing (e.g., fermentation, DSP) | **4.28** |
| Mixed (typical bioprocess) | **3.63** |

### 2.2.2 Six-Tenths Rule (Scale-Up Cost Estimation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** When scaling equipment size, the cost does NOT scale linearly. It scales as the **0.6 power** of the size ratio. This is because doubling the size of a vessel does *not* double its cost.

$$C_{B} = C_{A} \times \left(\frac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}\right)^{0.6}$$

Where:
- $C_B$ = Cost of the larger unit
- $C_A$ = Cost of the smaller (known) unit
- $S_B / S_A$ = Size ratio (capacity ratio)

### 2.2.3 The Hand Factor Method (More Detailed)

$$C_{FCI} = C_{eq} \times \left(1 + f_{inst} + f_{pip} + f_{elec} + f_{bldg} + f_{eng}\right)$$

Where $f_i$ are empirical multipliers:
- Installation $f_{inst}$ = **0.35**
- Piping $f_{pip}$ = **0.40**
- Electrical $f_{elec}$ = **0.10**
- Buildings $f_{bldg}$ = **0.20**
- Engineering $f_{eng}$ = **0.30**
- Sum ≈ **2.35** multiplier

### 2.2.4 Working Capital

$$C_{WC} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times C_{FCI}$$

### 2.2.5 Total Capital Investment

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC}$$

---

## 2.3 Pillar 2 — Operating Cost (OpEx) Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Cost (Annual)** = Direct Costs + Indirect Costs + Fixed Costs + General Expenses.

### 2.3.1 Direct Manufacturing Costs

$$C_{Direct} = C_{RM} + C_{util} + C_{labor} + C_{maint} + C_{sup}$$

| Sub-cost | Typical % of Annual OpEx | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Materials $C_{RM}$ | **20–35 %** | Media, antibiotics, resins, buffers |
| Utilities $C_{util}$ | **15–25 %** | WFI water, steam, electricity |
| Labor $C_{labor}$ | **15–25 %** | Operators, supervisors |
| Maintenance $C_{maint}$ | **5–10 %** | Resin replacement, membrane swap |
| Consumables $C_{sup}$ | **2–5 %** | Filters, gloves, columns |

### 2.3.2 Indirect, Fixed, and General Expenses

$$C_{Indirect} = \text{Overheads, R\&D, Taxes, Insurance, Depreciation}$$

### 2.3.3 Cost of Manufacture (COM) Equation

$$C_{COM} = C_{Direct} + C_{Indirect} + C_{Fixed} + C_{Gen}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS):** $C_{COGS} = C_{COM} / (\text{Annual Production Output})$

---

## 2.4 Pillar 3 — Profitability Indicators

### 2.4.1 Return on Investment (ROI)

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Annual Net Profit}}{C_{TCI}} \times 100 \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A biotech plant is considered *commercially viable* if **ROI > 15 %** for pharmaceuticals, **ROI > 25 %** for industrial enzymes.

### 2.4.2 Pay-Back Time (PBT)

$$t_{PB} = \frac{C_{TCI}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}} \quad [\text{years}]$$

### 2.4.3 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$
- $i$ = Discount rate (typical 10–15 %)
- $N$ = Project life (typical 10–15 years for pharma)

### 2.4.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The value of $i$ for which $NPV = 0$. Solve:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i^{*})^{t}} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If IRR > minimum acceptable rate of return (MARR), accept the project. For biosimilars, MARR ≈ **20 %**.

---

## 2.5 Sensitivity Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Analysis** quantifies *how much* the output (e.g., ROI) changes when one input (e.g., raw material cost, selling price, yield) is perturbed by a small percentage. It identifies the **critical variables** that the engineer must control tightly.

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\%\Delta \text{Output}}{\%\Delta \text{Input}}$$

| Variable | Typical Sensitivity for Insulin Process |
|---|---|
| Selling Price | **HIGH (1.5–2.5)** ← most sensitive |
| Fermentation Yield | **HIGH (1.0–1.5)** |
| Resin Lifetime | MEDIUM (0.5–0.8) |
| Utility Cost | LOW (0.2–0.4) |
| Labor Cost | LOW (0.1–0.3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tornado Diagram** is used to visualize the relative sensitivity of variables — the longest bar is the most sensitive input.

---

## 2.6 Cash Flow Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cash Flow ($CF_t$)** = Money Inflow − Money Outflow in year $t$.

| Year | Typical Activity | Cash Flow Sign |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (Construction) | Land, equipment, FCI | **Large Negative** |
| 1 (Start-up) | Initial inventory, training | Negative |
| 2–10 (Operation) | Revenue − OpEx − Tax | **Positive (growing)** |
| 11 (Salvage) | Plant salvage, working capital recovery | Positive |
| 12 (Closure) | Decommissioning, cleanup | Negative |

---

## 2.7 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — at least 70 % of Part B questions draw from these equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lang Capital Cost | $C_{FCI} = F_{lang} \cdot \sum C_{eq}$ | ₹ / USD |
| 2 | Six-Tenths Rule | $C_{B} = C_{A} (S_{B}/S_{A})^{0.6}$ | ₹ / USD |
| 3 | Hand Method | $C_{FCI} = C_{eq}(1 + \sum f_{i})$ | ₹ / USD |
| 4 | Working Capital | $C_{WC} = 0.15 \cdot C_{FCI}$ | ₹ |
| 5 | TCI | $C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC}$ | ₹ |
| 6 | Power Scale-Up (constant $P/V$) | $P_{B}/V_{B} = P_{A}/V_{A}$ | W/m³ |
| 7 | $k_L a$ Scale-Up | $k_{L}a \propto (P/V)^{0.56} \cdot v_{s}^{0.7}$ | 1/s |
| 8 | Annual OpEx | $C_{Op} = C_{RM} + C_{util} + C_{lab} + C_{mt}$ | ₹/year |
| 9 | Cost of Goods | $COGS = C_{COM}/N_{prod}$ | ₹/unit |
| 10 | ROI | $ROI = (\text{Profit}/TCI) \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Pay-Back | $t_{PB} = TCI/\text{Annual Cash Flow}$ | years |
| 12 | NPV | $NPV = \sum CF_t/(1+i)^t$ | ₹ |
| 13 | IRR | $i^{*}$ where $NPV = 0$ | % |
| 14 | Sensitivity | $S = (\%\Delta Y)/(\%\Delta X)$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Taxed Profit | $P_{net} = P_{gross}(1-T)$ with $T = 0.30$ | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **ROI** (ratio of annual profit to capital) with **IRR** (discount rate that zeroes NPV). They are completely different mathematical objects.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma Industry:** Used to justify the launch of biosimilars (e.g., insulin glargine, etanercept).
- **Industrial Enzymes:** Justifies cellulase or amylase production at 50,000 L scale.
- **Vaccines:** Determines whether mRNA vaccine production is profitable at ₹200/dose.
- **Startup Decisions:** Investors demand ROI > 25 % and IRR > 20 % before funding a bioprocess scale-up.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Recombinant Human Insulin Plant Economics

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU Standard):**
> Design a recombinant Human Insulin plant using *E. coli* K-12 with the following data:
>
> - Annual demand: **500 kg of pure Insulin per year**
> - Overall process yield (fermentation → purified API): **25 %**
> - Plant uptime: **330 days/year**
> - Insulin selling price: **₹ 25,000 / gram**
> - Lang factor (fluid process): **4.28**
> - Discount rate: **i = 12 %**
> - Project life: **10 years**
> - Tax rate: **30 %**
> - Raw material cost: **35 % of operating cost**
> - Utility cost: **20 % of operating cost**
> - Labor cost: **20 % of operating cost**
> - Maintenance: **10 % of operating cost**
> - Other (insurance, R&D, overhead): **15 % of operating cost**

**Major Equipment Cost Data:**

| Equipment | Size | Purchased Cost (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|
| Fermenter | 50 m³ | 60 |
| Centrifuge (disk-stack) | — | 25 |
| Homogenizer | — | 18 |
| Chromatography skid (3 columns) | — | 35 |
| UF/DF system | — | 20 |
| Crystallizer + Lyophilizer | — | 28 |
| Clean-in-place (CIP) + Utilities | — | 14 |
| **Total Equipment Cost** | — | **200 lakh** |

---

### Step 1 — Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) using Lang Factor

We apply the Lang equation for a **fluid-processing** bioprocess (fermentation, chromatography, filtration):

$$C_{FCI} = F_{lang} \times \sum C_{eq}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$C_{FCI} = 4.28 \times 200 \text{ lakh}$$

$$\boxed{C_{FCI} = 856 \text{ lakh} = ₹ 8.56 \text{ crore}}$$

**Textual interpretation:**
The Lang factor of 4.28 means that for every ₹1 of equipment cost, the total plant costs ₹4.28 — this 4.28× multiplier covers installation (35 %), piping (40 %), electrical (10 %), buildings (20 %), engineering (30 %), and contingencies (~93 %). Hence ₹200 lakh of equipment becomes a ₹856 lakh plant.

---

### Step 2 — Working Capital (WC)

Apply the empirical working capital rule of **15 % of FCI** for biotech facilities:

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{FCI}$$

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times 856 \text{ lakh}$$

$$\boxed{C_{WC} = 128.4 \text{ lakh}}$$

**Textual interpretation:**
Working capital is the money tied up in raw-material inventory, in-process batches, finished goods, and cash reserves needed to keep the plant running before first sale is realized. For biopharma, it is typically held for ~3 months of operations.

---

### Step 3 — Total Capital Investment (TCI)

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC}$$

$$C_{TCI} = 856 + 128.4 \text{ lakh}$$

$$\boxed{C_{TCI} = 984.4 \text{ lakh} = ₹ 9.844 \text{ crore}}$$

---

### Step 4 — Annual Revenue

Annual Insulin production = **500 kg = 5,00,000 grams**.

$$R_{annual} = 5,00,000 \text{ g} \times ₹25{,}000/\text{g}$$

$$R_{annual} = ₹ 1.25 \times 10^{10}$$

$$\boxed{R_{annual} = ₹ 125 \text{ crore/year}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Annual Operating Cost (OpEx)

We need an additional data point. Assume **Operating Cost = 30 % of Revenue** (typical for biopharma after fermentation yields are good).

$$C_{Op} = 0.30 \times ₹ 125 \text{ crore}$$

$$\boxed{C_{Op} = ₹ 37.5 \text{ crore/year}}$$

**Verify sub-categories:**

| Sub-cost | % | ₹ crore/year |
|---|---|---|
| Raw materials | 35 % | **13.125** |
| Utilities (WFI, steam, power) | 20 % | **7.500** |
| Labor | 20 % | **7.500** |
| Maintenance | 10 % | **3.750** |
| Other (insurance, R&D, overhead) | 15 % | **5.625** |
| **Total** | 100 % | **37.500** |

---

### Step 6 — Gross Profit

$$P_{gross} = R_{annual} - C_{Op}$$

$$P_{gross} = 125 - 37.5 \text{ crore}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gross} = ₹ 87.5 \text{ crore/year}}$$

---

### Step 7 — Net Profit (after 30 % Tax)

$$P_{net} = P_{gross} \times (1 - T)$$

$$P_{net} = 87.5 \times (1 - 0.30)$$

$$P_{net} = 87.5 \times 0.70$$

$$\boxed{P_{net} = ₹ 61.25 \text{ crore/year}}$$

---

### Step 8 — Return on Investment (ROI)

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{P_{net}}{C_{TCI}} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{61.25 \text{ crore}}{9.844 \text{ crore}} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{ROI} = 622.2 \%}$$

**Interpretation:**
ROI is astronomically high because TCI (capital) is a one-time investment while annual revenue is recurring. Realistic industrial ROI for pharma is 30–60 %; the inflated value here is because we used a representative small plant with a high-margin drug. Board exams accept this calculation as long as the method is correct.

---

### Step 9 — Pay-Back Time (PBT)

$$t_{PB} = \frac{C_{TCI}}{P_{net}}$$

$$t_{PB} = \frac{9.844 \text{ crore}}{61.25 \text{ crore/year}}$$

$$t_{PB} = 0.1607 \text{ year}$$

$$\boxed{t_{PB} \approx 2 \text{ months}}$$

---

### Step 10 — Sensitivity Analysis (Selling Price)

Suppose selling price drops by **10 %** (from ₹25,000 to ₹22,500 / g):

| Metric | Base | After 10 % Drop | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue (₹ crore) | 125.00 | 112.50 | −10 % |
| OpEx (₹ crore) | 37.50 | 37.50 | 0 % |
| Gross Profit | 87.50 | 75.00 | −14.3 % |
| Net Profit | 61.25 | 52.50 | −14.3 % |
| ROI | 622 % | 533 % | −14.3 % |

$$\text{Sensitivity (Price)} = \frac{-14.3}{-10} = \boxed{1.43}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1 % drop in selling price causes a 1.43 % drop in ROI. Therefore, **selling price is a high-sensitivity variable** and must be carefully protected through market contracts and pricing strategy.

---

### Step 11 — Cash Flow Table (Simplified)

| Year | Activity | Cash Flow (₹ crore) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Plant Construction + WC | **−9.844** |
| 1 | Start-up (60 % capacity) | +30.00 |
| 2 | 80 % capacity | +45.00 |
| 3–9 | Full capacity | +61.25 |
| 10 | Full + Salvage | +66.25 |

---

### Step 12 — Net Present Value (NPV) at i = 12 %

$$NPV = -9.844 + \frac{30}{(1.12)^{1}} + \frac{45}{(1.12)^{2}} + \sum_{t=3}^{9} \frac{61.25}{(1.12)^{t}} + \frac{66.25}{(1.12)^{10}}$$

Computing each term:

$$NPV = -9.844 + 26.786 + 35.879 + 217.42 + 45.18$$

$$\boxed{NPV = ₹ 315.4 \text{ crore}}$$

Since NPV is **strongly positive**, the project is accepted.

---

## 3.2 Six-Tenths Rule — Scale-Up Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A pilot centrifuge costs ₹ 18 lakh for 100 L/h throughput. Estimate the cost of the production-scale centrifuge at 2000 L/h.

Apply the six-tenths rule:

$$C_{B} = C_{A} \times \left(\frac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}\right)^{0.6}$$

$$C_{B} = 18 \times \left(\frac{2000}{100}\right)^{0.6}$$

$$C_{B} = 18 \times (20)^{0.6}$$

Computing the power:

$$20^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \cdot \ln(20)} = e^{0.6 \times 2.9957} = e^{1.7974} = 6.034$$

$$C_{B} = 18 \times 6.034 = ₹ 108.6 \text{ lakh}$$

$$\boxed{C_{centrifuge, prod} = ₹ 1.086 \text{ crore}}$$

**Interpretation:** A 20× throughput increase only multiplies the cost by 6×, confirming the economy-of-scale principle in DSP.

---

## 3.3 Power/Volume Scale-Up for Bioreactor

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $P/V$ constant?** Oxygen transfer rate, mixing time, and shear are all proportional to the power-per-unit-volume. Keeping $P/V$ constant ensures the same physiological environment for *E. coli* cells at any scale.

$$P_{B} = P_{A} \times \left(\frac{V_{B}}{V_{A}}\right) \times \left(\frac{P/V \text{ constant}}{1}\right)$$

Example: Lab fermenter 10 L, $P_A$ = 50 W. Scale to 50,000 L with constant $P/V$:

$$P_{B} = 50 \times (50{,}000/10) = 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ W} = 250 \text{ kW}$$

But impeller tip speed must also be capped (Newtonian regime, $N \cdot D$ constant in turbulent regime):

$$N_{B} = N_{A} \times \left(\frac{D_{A}}{D_{B}}\right)^{2/3}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Criteria Summary

| Metric | Formula | Accept Value | Our Project Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ROI | $P_{net}/TCI \times 100$ | ≥ 25 % | 622 % | ✅ Accept |
| PBT | $TCI/P_{net}$ | ≤ 3 years | 2 months | ✅ Accept |
| NPV at 12 % | $\sum CF/(1+i)^t$ | > 0 | +315.4 cr | ✅ Accept |
| IRR | $i^{*}$ such that NPV=0 | > 20 % | >100 % | ✅ Accept |
| Sensitivity (price) | $\%\Delta Y/\%\Delta X$ | < 1.0 | 1.43 | ⚠️ Monitor |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Downstream Process Economics" and state why it is critical in bioprocess commercialization.**

**Model Answer:**
Downstream Process Economics (DPE) is the systematic evaluation of capital and operating costs of all purification operations (centrifugation, chromatography, filtration, crystallization) carried out after upstream fermentation to recover and purify the target bioproduct.

It is critical because downstream processing accounts for **60–80 %** of total bioproduct cost. Without economic evaluation, a technically successful laboratory process cannot be translated into a profitable commercial product. It determines whether the ROI, NPV, and pay-back time meet investor benchmarks.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**What is the "Six-Tenths Rule" for scale-up cost estimation? Write the formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The Six-Tenths Rule is an empirical cost-scaling law that states the cost of a process unit scales as the **0.6 power** of its size ratio. It is used to estimate the cost of larger equipment from a known smaller unit.

$$C_{B} = C_{A} \times \left(\frac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}\right)^{0.6}$$

For example, doubling the size multiplies the cost by only $2^{0.6} = 1.516$, not 2 — reflecting economy-of-scale in industrial plants.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Lang Factor method** and **Hand Factor method** for estimating the fixed capital investment of a recombinant Insulin plant. Use an example with total equipment cost ₹ 200 lakh and Lang factor 4.28. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **ROI, Pay-Back Time, and NPV (at i = 12 %)** for an Insulin plant producing 500 kg/yr at ₹ 25,000/g, TCI = ₹ 9.844 crore, OpEx = ₹ 37.5 crore/yr, tax = 30 %, and 10-year project life. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Lang Factor Method:**
The Lang factor method is a short-cut capital cost estimation technique. The fixed capital investment is calculated by multiplying the total delivered equipment cost by a dimensionless Lang factor.

$$C_{FCI} = F_{lang} \times \sum C_{eq}$$

For a fluid-processing bioprocess (fermentation + chromatography + filtration):

$$F_{lang} = 4.28$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

$$C_{FCI} = 4.28 \times 200 = 856 \text{ lakh} = ₹ 8.56 \text{ crore}$$

**Hand Factor Method (more detailed):**
The Hand method multiplies equipment cost by sum of installation, piping, electrical, building, and engineering factors:

$$C_{FCI} = C_{eq}(1 + f_{inst} + f_{pip} + f_{elec} + f_{bldg} + f_{eng})$$

Using standard factors (0.35, 0.40, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30):

$$C_{FCI} = 200 \times (1 + 1.35) = 200 \times 2.35 = ₹ 470 \text{ lakh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Hand method gives ₹4.7 crore because it excludes the 93 % contingency that Lang's 4.28 includes implicitly. Lang is a more conservative estimate — preferred at the **order-of-magnitude** stage of a project (KTU expects Lang at 4.28 for fluid processes).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Revenue:**
$R = 5,00,000 \text{ g} \times 25{,}000 = ₹ 125 \text{ crore/yr}$

**Step 2 — Net Profit:**
$P_{gross} = 125 - 37.5 = 87.5 \text{ cr}$
$P_{net} = 87.5 \times (1 - 0.30) = 61.25 \text{ cr/yr}$

**Step 3 — ROI:**
$$\text{ROI} = \frac{61.25}{9.844} \times 100 = 622.2 \%$$

**Step 4 — Pay-Back Time:**
$$t_{PB} = \frac{9.844}{61.25} = 0.161 \text{ yr} \approx 2 \text{ months}$$

**Step 5 — NPV at 12 % over 10 years (assume steady CF):**
$$NPV = -9.844 + \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{61.25}{(1.12)^t}$$

Sum of PV annuity factor for 10 years at 12 % = **5.6502**

$$NPV = -9.844 + (61.25 \times 5.6502) = -9.844 + 346.07 = +₹ 336.2 \text{ crore}$$

**Conclusion:** ROI > 25 %, PBT < 3 yrs, NPV > 0 ⇒ **Project is commercially viable** and should be approved for commercialization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Sample]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Sensitivity Analysis** with reference to a recombinant Insulin plant. Identify the THREE most sensitive variables and justify with the formula:
$$S = \frac{\%\Delta \text{Output}}{\%\Delta \text{Input}}$$ **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pilot centrifuge costs ₹ 18 lakh at 100 L/h capacity. Estimate the cost of a production centrifuge of 2000 L/h using the **Six-Tenths Rule**. Also discuss the **scale-up criteria** for maintaining oxygen transfer rate ($k_L a$) in an *E. coli* fermenter scaled from 10 L to 50,000 L. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Sensitivity Analysis Definition:**
Sensitivity Analysis quantifies how a percentage change in one input variable affects the percentage change in an output (typically ROI or NPV). The sensitivity coefficient is:

$$S = \frac{\%\Delta Y}{\%\Delta X}$$

**Three Most Sensitive Variables for Insulin Plant:**

1. **Selling Price of Insulin** — Sensitivity ≈ **1.4** (HIGH). A 1 % drop in price causes 1.4 % drop in ROI. This is the most sensitive variable because revenue scales linearly with price while costs are mostly fixed.

2. **Fermentation Yield** — Sensitivity ≈ **1.2** (HIGH). A 1 % drop in $Y_{p/s}$ (product per substrate) reduces final API output by 1.2 %, cascading through the entire DSP.

3. **Resin Lifetime** — Sensitivity ≈ **0.65** (MEDIUM). Ion-exchange resins cost ₹ 5–15 lakh each and degrade over 50–200 cycles. Reducing lifetime by 10 % raises OpEx by ~6.5 %.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineers must lock down high-sensitivity variables through long-term contracts (price), optimized media (yield), and validated regeneration protocols (resin life).**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Six-Tenths Rule Application:**

$$C_{B} = 18 \times (2000/100)^{0.6} = 18 \times 20^{0.6}$$

$20^{0.6} = 6.034$

$$\boxed{C_{B} = ₹ 108.6 \text{ lakh}}$$

**Scale-Up Criteria for $k_L a$ in Fermenter:**

For *E. coli* (high oxygen demand, $q_{O_2} \approx 10 \text{ mmol/g·h}$), the volumetric mass transfer coefficient $k_L a$ must remain constant across scales:

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P}{V}\right)^{0.56} \cdot v_s^{0.7}$$

Where:
- $P/V$ = Power per unit volume (W/m³)
- $v_s$ = Superficial gas velocity (m/s)
- $K$ = Geometric constant

**Scaling from 10 L to 50,000 L:**
- Maintain $P/V$ constant ⇒ $P_B = P_A \times (V_B/V_A)$
- Lab $P_A$ = 50 W ⇒ Production $P_B$ = 50 × 5000 = 250,000 W = **250 kW**
- Adjust sparger and impeller design to keep $v_s$ at ~0.05 m/s
- Validate dissolved oxygen (DO) ≥ 30 % saturation at all scales

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus fact for KTU:** $k_L a$ for *E. coli* fermentation must be ≥ **200 h⁻¹** to avoid oxygen limitation and shift to mixed-acid fermentation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (board examiners explicitly check these):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Lang factor for Solid vs. Fluid processes.** Fluid = **4.28**; Solid = **3.10**. Using the wrong one costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to include Working Capital.** TCI = FCI + WC. Many students stop at FCI and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Using 365 days** for plant uptime instead of **330 days (90.4 %)**. Industry standard accounts for shutdown, maintenance, and CIP cycles.
> 4. **Mixing ROI with IRR.** ROI is profit/capital (simple ratio). IRR is the discount rate that zeroes NPV (iterative). They are NOT the same.
> 5. **Using Gross Profit instead of Net Profit** in ROI. Tax MUST be deducted: $P_{net} = P_{gross}(1-T)$ with $T = 30\%$.
> 6. **Skipping the salvage value** in the year-10 cash flow for NPV calculation.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in the Six-Tenths Rule: both costs must be in same currency; size ratio is dimensionless.
> 8. **Sensitivity formula sign confusion.** Always report the absolute value of the sensitivity ratio.

---

> ✅ **Hand Factor**: $C_{FCI} = C_{eq}(1 + \sum f_i)$ with multipliers 0.35, 0.40, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30.
> ✅ **Working Capital = 10–20 % of FCI** (typically 15 %).
> ✅ **TCI = FCI + WC** — the true capital cost.
> ✅ **Plant uptime = 330 days/year** (industry benchmark).
> ✅ **OpEx categories**: Raw Material, Utilities, Labor, Maintenance, Consumables, Overheads.
> ✅ **ROI = Net Profit / TCI × 100 %** — accept if > 25 %.
> ✅ **Pay-Back Time = TCI / Annual Cash Flow** — accept if < 3 years.
> ✅ **NPV = $\sum CF_t/(1+i)^t$** — accept if > 0.
> ✅ **IRR** = discount rate that zeroes NPV — accept if > MARR (20 %).
> ✅ **Sensitivity = $(\%\Delta Y) / (\%\Delta X)$** — high-sensitivity variables need tight control.
> ✅ **Cash flow stages**: Year 0 (Construction, −TCI) → Years 1–N (Positive OpEx profit) → Year N+1 (Salvage).
> ✅ **Power/Volume (P/V) constant** for bioreactor scale-up.
> ✅ **$k_L a \propto (P/V)^{0.56} v_s^{0.7}$** for oxygen transfer preservation.
> ✅ **Insulin market price** ≈ ₹25,000/g; tax rate = 30 %; project life = 10 years.
